normalized_text: Since it has been introduced, the MMC is recognized as highly scalable,, flexible and reliable topology. As can be seen, the MMC consists of SMs (or cells), which are normally found in Half-Bridge (HB) or Full-Bridge (FB) configurations, however, other types of SMs have also been reported in the literature. Series connection of SMs, combined with an inductor labeled with Lbr, is referred to as the branch (sometimes, the term arm is also used). Two branches (arms) form the leg: By stacking the SMs in series, voltage handling capabilities of the MMC can be effortlessly increased, meaning that it represents a suitable candidate for applications in voltage range being of interest for FUNDRES project (i.e. 9 kVDC). However, realization of the MMC prototype is not a trivial task and several technical challenges must be solved even before commissioning the converter as a whole. As the MMC is also recognized for its complex control, testing of different control schemes must be performed in the safest possible environment. For the matter, a digital twin, described also in the previous reports, has been built and successfully employed to test various operating scenarios of the MMC. In parallel, development of and testing of the SM, cabinet and other parts surrounding the converter had been ongoing. This documents provides a thorough insight into the structure of the realized MMC SM. Step-by-step explanation on the procedure followed during the SM testing is provided, revealing the complexity of the process. Results obtained during the SM testing are included and discussed in detail. As indicated in the last part of this document, testing of the converter as a whole could not be performed due to the unfortunate worldwide shortage of semiconductors. Hence, this document focuses predominantly on the SM, as it represents the basic building block of the MMC.
statements: The MMC is recognized as highly scalable, flexible, and reliable topology.
The MMC consists of SMs (or cells) normally found in Half-Bridge (HB) or Full-Bridge (FB) configurations.
Series connection of SMs combined with an inductor labeled Lbr is referred to as the branch.
Two branches form the leg.
Stacking the SMs in series increases the MMC's voltage handling capabilities.
A digital twin was built and successfully employed to test various operating scenarios of the MMC.
Testing of the converter as a whole could not be performed due to a worldwide shortage of semiconductors.
compacted_text: The MMC is a scalable, flexible, and reliable topology. It consists of submodules (SMs, or cells), normally in half-bridge (HB) or full-bridge (FB) configurations, though other SM types have been reported. Series-connected SMs with an inductor Lbr form a branch (or arm), and two branches form a leg. By stacking SMs in series, the MMC voltage handling capability can be increased, making it suitable for the FUNDRES project voltage range of 9 kVDC. The MMC has complex control, so different control schemes were tested in a digital twin built for safe testing of operating scenarios. Development and testing of the SM, cabinet, and other parts were ongoing. This document focuses on the realized MMC SM, its testing procedure, and results. Testing of the converter as a whole could not be performed due to the worldwide shortage of semiconductors.

normalized_text: The use of bipolar SMs provides the possibility to generate negative voltage across the MMC DC terminals. Such a feature comes in handy especially if short circuits occurring at the converter DC side are to be countered. Although various topologies of bipolar SMs have been reported in the literature, the SM being the subject of this document is realized as an independent FB converter, mainly due to simplicity reasons. Although simple in terms of its topology, practical implementation of an MMC SM being used in Medium Voltage (MV) application poses significant integration challenges, such as the need for an auxiliary power supply, proper insulation coordination, employment of internal protection, proper thermal design, current and voltage measurements, certain communication links with upper level controllers, etc. Utilizing commercially available Low Voltage (LV) power components was the foundation for the converter design, and it was mainly driven by low cost design targets. Therefore, bipolar property of the SM has been achieved with 1200 V IGBT HB modules. The choice of the 1200 V rated IGBT module and 5 kVDC voltage rating of a single MMC, imposed the need for N = 8 SMs per single MMC branch, with each SM average voltage being set as 625 V. The structure of the realized SM has several electronic circuits performing different functions, which are organized and split between two interconnected PCBs: Power PCB and Control PCB.
statements: The use of bipolar SMs enables generating negative voltage across the MMC DC terminals.
Bipolar SMs can help counter short circuits occurring at the converter DC side.
The SM in this document is realized as an independent FB converter mainly due to simplicity reasons.

Practical implementation of an MMC SM in Medium Voltage applications poses significant integration challenges including an auxiliary power supply, insulation coordination, internal protection, thermal design, measurements, and communication links with upper level controllers.
The converter design was based on utilizing commercially available Low Voltage power components driven by low cost design targets.
The realized SM is organized into two interconnected PCBs: a Power PCB and a Control PCB.
compacted_text: Bipolar SMs can generate negative voltage across the MMC DC terminals, useful for countering short circuits on the converter DC side. The SM in this document is realized as an independent FB converter for simplicity. Practical MV MMC SM implementation requires auxiliary power supply, insulation coordination, internal protection, thermal design, current and voltage measurements, and communication links with upper-level controllers. The converter design used commercially available LV power components for low cost; bipolar operation was achieved with 1200 V IGBT HB modules. With a 1200 V IGBT module and 5 kVDC single-MMC voltage rating, N = 8 SMs per MMC branch were required, with each SM average voltage set to 625 V. The realized SM is organized into two interconnected PCBs: Power PCB and Control PCB.

normalized_text: Overview of Tests In order to thoroughly test the SM, it is necessary to test the following: • SM control software state-machine and protection features (Single SM test) • Voltage, current and temperature measurements (Single SM test) • Open-loop voltage generation functionality (SM String Test) • Electrical and thermal stress - load capability (Heat-run test) Each one of the tests will be thoroughly explained, together with presented test results, in Section 7.2. gives an overview of different test procedures and steps involved. User can select in GUI a type of the test to be performed, which is communicated to AC 800PEC. State-machine implemented in the main controller executes appropriate set of actions, in accordance with the received command. • Purpose of the Single SM test is to verify both hardware and software functionalities of a single MMC SM. The test is controlled through GUI, and therefore it has first been developed on mHIL. Upon verification of the GUI functionality for the given test, it is then conducted on a real PEL MMC SM. Part of the test is the voltage, current and temperature calibration of the individual SMs.
statements: Thorough testing of the SM requires testing SM control software state-machine and protection features.
Thorough testing of the SM requires testing voltage, current, and temperature measurements.
Thorough testing of the SM requires testing open-loop voltage generation functionality.
A user can select a test type in the GUI, and this selection is communicated to AC 800PEC.
The purpose of the Single SM test is to verify both hardware and software functionalities of a single MMC SM.
compacted_text: To thoroughly test the SM, the following are tested: SM control software state-machine and protection features, voltage/current/temperature measurements, open-loop voltage generation, and electrical and thermal stress/load capability. The user selects the test type in the GUI, which is communicated to AC 800PEC; the main controller state-machine executes the appropriate actions. The Single SM test verifies hardware and software functionality of a single MMC SM and includes voltage, current, and temperature calibration of individual SMs. It is first developed on mHIL, then conducted on a real PEL MMC SM after GUI verification.

normalized_text: The following tests are carried out for each one of the SMs: • Over-voltage test: Each SM has its DC capacitor voltage measurement implemented as part of its HW and DSP SW. Since the nominal SM voltage in real operation is supposed to be around 650 V, and the employed DC link capacitors can normally withstand 800 V, it was decided that the over-voltage threshold shall be set to 770 V. Over-voltage protection is realized as part of the state-machine of the SM controller, implemented on DSP. Once the measured voltage surpasses the threshold, the SM state-machine goes into the so-called ”Free Wheel” state (state 20), which immediately suppresses the gate pulses to the IGBTs. In order to test the over-voltage protection functionality, MMC SM is connected. Controllable DC voltage source is supplying SM DC terminals, whereas an inductor load is connected to the AC terminals, acting as a load. SM state-machine goes into the so-called ”Free Wheel” state (state 20), which immediately suppresses the gate pulses to the IGBTs. In order to test the over-voltage protection functionality, MMC SM is connected. Controllable DC voltage source is supplying SM DC terminals, whereas an inductor load is connected to the AC terminals, acting as a load. relay and Transient Voltage Suppression (TVS) diodes. Activation of the bypass thyristor by the DSP of the SM (signal ”THY_ON”) provides the path to the AC terminal current, thus circumventing the SM. Experimental results are conducted to test this protection feature of the SM. In order to observe the activation of the bypass thyristor, the SM is supplied by the DC power supply from its AC terminals, once the DC voltage reaches 800 V, the max-voltage protection is activated, and bypasses the SM. It is important to mention that PWM was inactivate during this test. • TVS diodes test: TVS diodes are part of the hardware-based protection of the SM against excessively high voltages across its AC terminals. This is the ultimate protection layer against high voltages on the SM, and is foreseen to execute only in case the former protection mechanisms (over-voltage and max-voltage) fail to execute, TVS diodes are placed between the AC terminals of the SM, and their property is that they start conducting only if the voltage across them surpasses certain level. In principle, once the voltage across AC terminals surpasses certain threshold for the diode activation (in our case 830V-850V), bypass relay and bypass thyristor turn-on signals are activated and the SM is bypassed. In order to experimentally verify theoretical expectations regarding the activation voltage, the SM is tested under the same conditions as for the Max-voltage test. Before the test is conducted, max-voltage protection threshold was changed through GUI from 800 V to 870 V, in order to avoid activation of this protection feature.

Over-voltage protection is implemented in the DSP state-machine; when measured voltage exceeds the threshold, the SM enters Free Wheel state 20 and immediately suppresses IGBT gate pulses. For the max-voltage test, the SM is connected to a controllable DC voltage source at the DC terminals and an inductor load at the AC terminals; when DC voltage reaches 800 V, max-voltage protection is activated and bypasses the SM. PWM was inactive during this test. TVS diodes are the hardware-based ultimate protection against excessively high AC-terminal voltages and conduct only above a threshold; in the test, activation was expected at 830 V–850 V. normalized_text: DC power supply is abruptly increased from 650V to 830V, where the slope of the voltage rise in the DC link, and thus across the terminals, is limited by the output current of the DC power supply. Experimental results are presented, where it can be seen that the SM is bypassed when the voltage across the terminals has reached 830V. This experiment was repeated with DC power supply voltage being set to higher values, and the results show that the highest TVS chain activation voltage was 860V. This corresponds to the theoretical expectations and thus ensures proper protection of the SM. • Over-temperature test: Another protection feature of the SM state-machine is the over-temperature protection mechanism. Namely, the junction temperature of the IGBT module is monitored through the integration of the DSP temperature sensor and if it surpasses the defined threshold value (”OT”), the DSP will go into the state ”Free Wheel”, similarly to over-voltage protection mechanism. This would result in the IGBT gate pulses suppression. Due to the fact that the effect of this protection mechanism is the same as for the over-voltage, and that the measured temperature is not available as external signal to be monitored on the oscilloscope, the results are not shown here. However, this test serves the purpose for monitoring the junction temperature, where the objective was to verify IGBT gate pulses suppression once the temperature of the junction reaches defined threshold. Junction temperature can be monitored through GUI, since it is one of the variables that DSP exchanges with the main controller. Test results verified the effectiveness of this protection feature. • Over-current test: Current sensor is part of the MMC SM. It measures the AC terminals current, sends the information to the DSP, which further processes the measured current and sends it as a variable to the main controller. Over-current protection is always part of the protection logic in the main controller. However, due to the delays in communication between the DSP and the main controller, it is possible to have locally implemented over-current protection mechanism. One can observe that the over-current detection (”OC”) leads the state-machine into the state 20 (”Free-wheel”), similarly to the over-voltage and over-temperature protection actions. As a result, IGBT gate pulses are suppressed. normalized_text: into the state 20 (”Free-wheel”), similarly to the over-voltage and over-temperature protection actions. As a result, IGBT gate pulses are suppressed. Due to the fact that the excessively high current in the MMC can continue to flow through the anti-parallel diodes and thus damage them, the state-machine activates a timer after the moment when over-current is into the state 20 (”Free-wheel”), similarly to the over-voltage and over-temperature protection actions. As a result, IGBT gate pulses are suppressed. Due to the fact that the excessively high current in the MMC can continue to flow through the anti-parallel diodes and thus damage them, the state-machine activates a timer after the moment when over-current is first detected. If such a current persists for longer than the timer preset value (1ms in our case), the SM is bypassed. In order to experimentally test the over-current protection mechanism, the SM is supplied from the DC power supply directly connected to its DC capacitor, whereas an inductor is connected to the AC terminals of the SM. Terminal voltage reference (adjustable via GUI) is generated in the main controller and communicates to the SM, which realizes it with PWM modulation. Consequently, an AC current flows through the load inductor, and by modifying the terminal voltage reference, the load current changes accordingly. Therefore, over-current protection activation (at 65A) is provoked by increasing the terminal voltage reference, which resulted in the current rise, and eventually over-current protection activation. The behaviour of the SM was as expected, since the IGBT gate pulses were suppressed as soon as the over-current is detected. Residual inductor current continued to flow through the anti-parallel diodes, until it rectified back to the value. Another protection mechanism, which is activated in case when the over-current persists for longer than 1ms could not be tested in this arrangement, since no external current source was available, that would support the over-current state. • DESAT Test: Desaturation detection in IGBT and MOSFET semiconductors is a well-known protection feature found in most of the commercial gate-drivers. It is based on the measurement of the voltage drop across the device during its active period.

In addition to the aforementioned tests, additional tests are conducted as part of the Single SM test group. However, these test served to verify the functionality of certain software-based protection features of the SM, and involved switching or resoldering of certain components on the PCB. For that reason, these software-based protection features were tested only on one SM. • Gate-driver power supply failure test: The purpose of this test was to observe the behaviour of the SM in case when the gate-driver losses its power supply. Gate-driver is supplied by 16V output from the cross-regulated multiple output flyback converter. The gate-driver has built-in Under-Voltage Lock-Out (UVLO) feature, which suppresses the gate-pulses in case when the supply voltage drops below 10V. However, no information is sent to the DSP, in case when such event occurs. To explore the behaviour of the SM under these circumstances, the SM is supplied at its DC terminals and the load inductor is connected to the AC terminals. Sinusoidal voltage reference was set in GUI, which resulted in the sinusoidal current through the load inductor. Gate-driver supply is interrupted during the operation by opening the first switch. At the time instant t = 0.09s the firt switch is opened, which resulted in constant decrease of the voltage across the capacitor supplying the gate-driver. Once the supply voltage dropped below 10V, the UVLO protection mechanism suppresses the gate pulses to the affected IGBT. Since the information regarding the loss of gate-driver power supply is not transferred to the DSP, the remaining IGBTs continue the operation, which results in deteriorated load current.

heading_path: Main > Chapter 1 > Digital Twin Demonstration > 1.1 Introduction
normalized_text: Nowadays, in lots of countries ERPS are in attendance and it is on the nationwide future projects of construction. ERPS function is complex and entails dynamics, communication, and signaling, power converter, tracks, etc. for which DT could be a sensible platform for design, testing, and evaluation. Because of the high level of complexity in ERPS, computer-based simulators might not be able to carry out such comprehensive evaluation while DT could be applied so to improve the efficiency and safety of ERPS. Neglecting signaling system in ERPS, the main application of DT in this field can be categorized as: 1) Energy management: The ERPS is known as a huge high-power network connected to the grid with time-varying loads (trains). Besides, the integration of renewable energy sources (RES), and energy storage systems (ESS) make the energy control in such a system more complex. Accordingly, the energy management needs the continuous and dynamic coordination of various parts including: braking/motoring trains, traction substation auxiliary loads, ESSs and RES. The communication of different layers in such a network can be implemented by concept of internet of things (IoT) using DT technology. 2) Power flow analysis: Depending on the structure of ERPS type and traditional or reversible traction substation in the line, different power flow methods can be implemented. Meanwhile, auxiliary power flow controllers which are based on power electronics converter are a promising solution to facilitate power flow control in ERPS. Measuring powers in different parts of the network and sending information to aggregator aiming the optimized power flow requires DT concept implementation.

DT in this field can be categorized under power flow analysis, which involves measuring powers in different parts of the network and sending information to an aggregator for optimized power flow.

Measuring powers in different parts of the network and sending information to aggregator aiming the optimized power flow requires DT concept implementation. 3) Power quality monitoring: Electrical compatibility between upstream grid and ERPS is critical facing power quality and reliability issues. ERPSS are known as one of the most polluted network from the power quality perspective. Simulation of these issues in software it’s not possible due to the limitation of modeling blocks and CPU. Different resonance situations, transient events and EMI are among the complex phenomena which may need exact real data measurement to be modeled. This highlights the need for DT implementation. 4) Fault diagnostic and maintenance: Trains and electric multiple units which contain a control part, a power circuit, various sensors, etc. are exposed to the mechanical vibrations and electromagnetic interference. On the other side, traction converters, the power electronic switches and sensors are sensitive to the faults. Motivated by these issues, the analysis of fault diagnosis and maintenance in ERPS has become a critical subject which can alleviate consequently deviations and transients. Even though applying offline simulations can be helpful for analyzing unusual situation in a particular part, but it will be a difficult function to emulate various abnormalities and faults in the whole ERPS. Hence, there is an urgent need to use DT and real-time HIL in this area. 5) Condition monitoring: As mentioned before, on time identification of faults in ERPS is critical for the availability, reliability, and safety reasons. To decrease the failure risk in ERPS, it is important to obtain, accumulate, and procedure detailed data regarding the technical condition of equipment. It can provide prediction for any destruction to the electric equipment. Faults diagnosis and maintenance is the next step after ERPS inftrastructure condition monitoring. Among the ERPS equipment which may need condition monitoring it can be mention to the return circuit, track circuit, impedance bond and pantograph catenary including arcing, overheating and abrasion.

Among the ERPS equipment which may need condition monitoring it can be mention to the return circuit, track circuit, impedance bond and pantograph catenary including arcing, overheating and abrasion. 6) Timetable management: The management of trains traffic in ERPS especially in crowded areas commonly is according to the off-line timetable design. Providing such a timetable schedule may need several months to include many variable situations and all possible conflicts among trains. Using the concept of DT and real-time, trains traffic and movements are managed with exact stability to the timetable. It facilitates planning train routes, sequences, and stop/crossing times at stations, with the purpose of regulating the impacted schedules from disturbances and decreasing negative consequences. 7) Operating profile optimization: In ERPSs, the energy efficiency is significantly impressed by the train operating profile including timetables and the applied driving modes. The operating profile optimization concerns the train control issues, for instance, optimizing the order of driving modes including accelerating, cruising, coasting, and braking together with the exchanging points between the modes, aiming of minimizing energy consumption and increasing system efficiency. It requires a dynamic model of the train and real-time measurement of system for energy calculations. This can be executed by concept of internet of things (IoT) using DT technology. In this project, a DT of future 9kV DC system is proposed using MATLAB software integrating distributed energy sources and EV charging infrastructures. In the proposed system, an algorithm is modified to accept real-data from physical railway system and simulate DT model based on the real data. The overall architecture of implementing digital twin for 9kV DC railway line is demonstrated. The used cases studies for modeling and for contribution of digital twin will be divided into 3 categories: • Low-level based on simplified model to study various elements for long time. • Mid-level for use case to analyze the impact on the AC mains and on the other elements, e.g. renewable generators and charging infrastructures. • High level to study transient events and protection strategies. The modelling of power flows in different scenarios contribute to the digital twin by: • Giving the performances of the future DC Railway electrification system; • Allowing to find the sizing power and the overload degree for the various power

Model Verification In the following, the input profiles for Digital Twin of electric railway power system at one TPSS is explained. The results which are used for Digital Twin are power outputs of renewable energy sources including PV panels and wind turbines that are presented. Also the load profile of electric vehicle is. impacts of RES integration into proposed 9 kV DC hub, the line situation has been modeled in normal working hours using real data as inputs. It is obvious from the measured power of TPSSs that integrating RES to the line decreases the contracted supplied power by substation. Depending on the weather situation and ratio of RES power to the system power the effect can be enhanced. In the other side, the extra power injected by RES can mitigate voltage drop. The other parameter which has been extracted from the model, is the amount of regenerative braking energy (RBE) in the stop station. From the measured power in TSS6 (Rome), it is obvious that for each train stop the negative power generated by braking can reach 8 MW. If monitoring mode trains exist close to the braking section some of the RBE can be consumed by them. However the extra RBE can either be stored by ESS or it can be returned to the network through bidirectional MMC based AC/DC substations.

heading_path: Main > Chapter 1 > Digital Twin Demonstration > 1.7 Conclusion
normalized_text: Electric Railway as a high-power load system in power grids known as a key technology in transportation industry. Due to the complexity and breadth of such networks, executing an exact simulation as an integral part of this technology plays a crucial role to obtain reliable and secure operations based on the performance of real physical system. In this context, implementation of Digital Twin (DT) can ensure the correct operation of monitoring and control function. In this project, an approach is presented to implement DT concept both at equipment and system level for future 9 kV MVDC systems integrating RES. It has been shown that DT capabilities to describe the actual and probable future state-run, makes it an unavoidable resolution for ERPS. The development of digital twin technology in electric railway power systems evaluated considering different sections of electric multiple unit, catenary, traction power substation and multi-train optimization. Utilizing DT modeling, the amount of generated energy by RES absorbed in trains, power flow analysis with and without regenerative braking energy, Power, voltage and current profile of substations, analysis of voltage drops with and without RES across the line and some other measurements related to the sensitivity analysis and transient events can be reached. The results showed that, the high level of complexity in ERPS, which may cause simulators not to be able to carry out such comprehensive evaluation could be addressed by DT. Since the implementation of the digital twin concept in ERPS as a future trend can be a promising technology to overcome limitations and complexities.
statements: Electric railway is described as a high-power load system in power grids and a key technology in the transportation industry.
Executing an exact simulation is presented as crucial for obtaining reliable and secure operations based on the performance of real physical systems.
Digital Twin (DT) implementation is presented as ensuring correct operation of monitoring and control functions.
An approach is presented to implement DT at both equipment and system levels for future 9 kV MVDC systems integrating RES.
DT modeling is described as enabling evaluation of generated energy by RES absorbed in trains and power flow analysis with and without regenerative braking energy.
The results state that DT can address the high level of complexity in ERPS that may prevent simulators from performing comprehensive evaluation.
compacted_text: Digital Twin (DT) can ensure correct monitoring and control in electric railway power systems (ERPS). The project presents DT implementation at equipment and system level for future 9 kV MVDC systems integrating RES. DT modeling was evaluated for electric multiple unit, catenary, traction power substation, and multi-train optimization, and can describe actual and probable future states. Using DT modeling, generated RES energy absorbed in trains, power flow with and without regenerative braking energy, substation power/voltage/current profiles, voltage drops with and without RES, sensitivity analysis, and transient events can be analyzed. The high complexity of ERPS may prevent simulators from carrying out such comprehensive evaluation, which DT can address.

normalized_text: Direct Current (DC) has several well-known advantages over Alternating Current (AC), but since DC transformation was only made possible by the introduction of semiconductors, AC had won the War of Currents in the late 19th century. Nowadays, DC can be efficiently transformed using converters; the advantages of DC stay, while the biggest disadvantage disappeared. The question whether AC or DC is the better choice newly arises, for already existing as well as new power transmission and distribution installations. International decarbonisation goals are the main driver for the high increase in Decentralized Energy Resources (DER), Renewable Energy Sources (RES), Energy Storage System (ESS) as well as Electric Vehicle (EV) and other new loads in home households. Their integration in in our energy supply system is leading towards new challenges in the low- and medium-voltage grid. The majority of these loads, storage systems and renewable energy generation units are internally operated in DC. Considering the advantages of DC for energy transmission such as increased transmission capacity, reduced conduction losses and voltage fluctuations or the facilitation of synchronisation, the introduction of DC in all grid voltage levels must be considered for secure and efficient electricity supply in the future. Furthermore, DC enables a more efficient way of integrating the increasing share of DC loads and generation units in the distribution grid thanks to the reduction of conversion stages. In summary, the main advantages of DC distribution systems include: • increased transmission capacity • increased supply radii (reduced conduction losses) • reduced voltage fluctuations • efficient integration of DC loads and generation (reduction of conversion stages) • optimal power flow control and short circuit current limitation • direct energy feedback in the grid by frequency-controlled loads with DC-intermediate circuit • reduced control variables in microgrid operation (no grid frequency/phase angle), facilitated synchronisation • environmental benefits (e.g. reduced CO2 emission) • economical benefits for already existing and potential use cases/pilot installations and grid configurations • grid resiliency (fault mitigation and avoidance of cascade effects) Following the development of High-Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) electric power transmission systems, there are efforts to use DC at lower voltage levels. Significant developments can be observed in the field of hybrid electric propulsion, and marine systems. DC-based low and medium-voltage distribution networks are now being investigated in many ongoing projects and studies as, among others. DC micro-, Low-Voltage (LV) and Medium-Voltage (MV) grids as

International decarbonisation goals are driving increased DER, RES, ESS, EVs, and other new household loads; many of these loads, storage systems, and renewable generation units are internally operated in DC, creating new challenges in low- and medium-voltage grids. DC-based low- and medium-voltage distribution networks, including DC micro-, LV and MV grids, are being investigated in ongoing projects and studies.

normalized_text: electric power transmission systems, there are efforts to use DC at lower voltage levels. Significant developments can be observed in the field of hybrid electric propulsion, and marine systems. DC-based low and medium-voltage distribution networks are now being investigated in many ongoing projects and studies as, among others. DC micro-, Low-Voltage (LV) and Medium-Voltage (MV) grids as well as hybrid ACDC distribution grids are promising solutions for future grid scenarios, especially considering the expected technological advances in DC equipment and components. This part explains on the general requirements on DC as an alternative to AC. As for the KPI domains presented in the previous chapter, technical, environmental, economical, social and legal requirements must be fulfilled for DC installations to be operated in the public grid. In general, these requirements do not differ for AC or DC systems from a customer point of view. They include high level requirements such as: • Reliability • Economical benefits • Environmentally friendly • Safe and Secure operation • Interoperability of components and systems. It must be ensured that DC installations are capable of at least fulfilling all requirements defined and set over the past century for AC grids. Additionally, DC specific requirements must be considered. Moreover, new challenges and requirements coupled with the hybridisation of the electric grid arise. The main differences between AC and DC must be identified and addressed in extended standardisation. Further requirements must be defined from a grid operator perspective to fulfil the high level customer requirements: • Interconnectivity with existing grid (transition from AC to hybrid ACDC) • Flexible and attractive pricing models • Renewable and local energy generation • Integration of local energy communities • Low environmental impacts such as CO2 emission • Standardisation. addressed in extended standardisation. Further requirements must be defined from a grid operator perspective to fulfil the high level customer requirements: • Interconnectivity with existing grid (transition from AC to hybrid ACDC) • Flexible and attractive pricing models • Renewable and local energy generation • Integration of local energy communities • Low environmental impacts such as CO2 emission • Standardisation. This part explains on the refurbishment of existing grids and requirements. Beside the potential benefits of DC systems in newly installed distribution systems, significant improvements in already existing infrastructure can be achieved by refurbishment of existing AC grids and lines to be operated in DC. Refurbishing existing AC distribution cables to operate under DC conditions can offer several advantages in terms of capacity enhancement, efficiency and flexibility in power and voltage control, among others. Especially where the existing AC grid reaches its limitations, the conversion of selected lines or feeders to DC can be economically and environmentally beneficial. The modification of an AC to a (partly) DC system can eliminate the need for construction replacement or reinforcement of lines, cable sections, power transformer etc. Pilot projects show how conversion of existing lines to DC can be economically beneficial, as presented in Section 2.2.1, or that the refurbishment of an AC line allows the continuous usage of a partially damaged cable due to single-phase failure by converting the system to bipolar DC operation, as discussed in section 2.2.9.

normalized_text: Any electric power grid needs protection in case of faults. Excessive over- and short-circuit currents must be avoided to prevent damage to equipment, avoid fire hazards, and other types of destruction of assets. In addition, selective protection is needed to ensure that only the faulty parts are disconnected from the grid, while the rest still remains operational. This section should give an introduction to DC grids with particular focus on the condition in an MVDC grid. 2.1 DC Grid Configurations: A DC grid or DC point-to-point connection can be configured in a couple of different ways with regard to grounding. This has a major impact on the transient voltages and currents during a short-circuit fault, and needs to be taken into account when designing the protection system, including the DC circuit breakers. It is currently not clear which grounding configuration will mostly be used for MVDC grids.

Grounding configuration in a DC grid affects transient voltages and currents during a short-circuit fault.

Excessive over- and short-circuit currents must be avoided to prevent equipment damage, fire hazards, and other destruction of assets. Selective protection is needed so only faulty parts are disconnected while the rest remains operational. In DC grids, grounding configuration has a major impact on transient voltages and currents during a short-circuit fault and must be considered when designing protection systems, including DC circuit breakers. It is currently not clear which grounding configuration will mostly be used for MVDC grids.

normalized_text: An MVDC grid is generally connected to the AC grid or another DC grid through power electronic converters. From a protection point of view, it is of interest to understand the behavior of such converters in case of a short-circuit fault in the MVDC grid. 2.2.1 Diode Rectifier: In case of a DC-side short circuit fault of an uncontrolled diode rectifier it will mostly behave as a short circuit also as seen from the AC side. The DC-side currents will rise rapidly, initially in a fashion similar to the shorting of a DC voltage source through an impedance. Eventually, if not interrupted or otherwise limited the currents will be limited by the AC-side reactances. In analytical expressions are derived for the DC-side fault currents of diode rectifiers, both transient and stationary. 2.2.2 Thyristor rectifiers: Without the use of special control algorithms to handle DC grid fault scenarios, a converter using thyristors as power switches behaves very much like a diode bridge rectifier when subjected to a DC-side short-circuit. Results of research have been published that describe methods using the control of thyristor switches to quench DC-side fault currents in marine DC applications. However, these rely on the reactance of on-board generators that considerably decreases the rate of change of fault currents and they are relatively slow (in the range of tens of milliseconds). It is estimated that the speed of such thyristor-based protection methods is too low for successfully protecting a VSC-based MVDC grid.

normalized_text: The basic Modular Multilevel Converter (MMC) topology resembles that of the ordinary two-level converter, but the semiconductor valves are replaced by strings of converter cells which act as controllable voltage sources. These voltage sources are controlled to provide unipolar voltages, each with both DC and AC components. The DC components are common to both phase arms in a phase leg, whereas the AC components have opposite signs. A great benefit of the MMC is that there is no capacitance needed across the DC poles like in a two-level converter. This means there is no immediate discharge of a capacitor in the case of a DC-side short circuit. However, as with the conventional two-level converter, it will lose control of all currents in the event of a DC-side short circuit. This MMC with the DC-side short-circuit behavior is similar to the two-level VSC. Notably, the unipolar voltages provided by the cell strings would only add to the DC-side short-circuit currents, and the cells must be blocked or bypassed. As a result, the converter will also behave as a rectifier of the AC side short-circuit currents, which will only be limited by the impedances on the AC side and the arm inductors. In practice, this means that a DC-side short-circuit requires blocking the power semiconductor switches in the cells for a short while if the DC-side fault current cannot be interrupted. This means that the converter will cease to operate normally and cannot even provide reactive power on the AC-side.

The MMC does not require capacitance across the DC poles like a two-level converter.
In a DC-side short circuit, the MMC loses control of all currents and behaves similarly to a two-level VSC.
During a DC-side short circuit, the converter must block or bypass the cells, and it ceases normal operation and cannot provide reactive power on the AC side.

normalized_text: A DC grid differs from an AC grid in that there is no limit to the fault current set by the grid impedance, other than the very low resistance. This means that the fault current will attain a very large steady-state value if it is not interrupted. Furthermore, an MVDC grid will always be fed by converters, either AC-DC converters connecting to AC grids, or DC-DC converters connecting to other DC grids. Depending on topology, some converters can limit the DC-side current at a fault, whereas others will lose control of the currents in case the DC voltage collapses. These circumstances have resulted in a couple of strategies for handling faults, which are reviewed below. 1) AC-side protection: In a DC grid interfacing AC grids using converters without DC-side control or blocking capability (e.g. two-level VSCs or MMCs with half-bridge cells), and where no fast DC breakers are installed, the only option in case of a DC fault is to block the converters and open the AC-side breakers. There is thus only one protection zone in the system and the grid discharges via the fault. This method is commonly used for point-to-point HVDC links, where selective fault detection is not meaningful. However, for a DC grid with several nodes this method will not be acceptable since vast transmission assets could be put out of operation due to a single fault. 2) DC circuit breaker selective protection: In case fast DCCBs are installed in all DC lines it can be possible to selectively isolate a fault on a line before the converters need to be blocked. This strategy resembles AC grid protection systems and can prevent significant disturbances on the healthy lines. An important difference is, however, that the entire process of detecting, locating, and isolating the fault has to be completed in much shorter time, typically a few milliseconds or less. This time is determined by several factors. First, the fault current cannot increase to excessive levels as otherwise a converter or any other equipment may be damaged. Second, it is desirable that no converter needs to block since this implies an interruption of both the active power and the reactive power that the converter to support a connected AC-grid by reactive power. This requirement generally puts a more severe limitation on the neutralization speed since the converter semiconductor switches tend to be the most sensitive part of the system. Studies reviews various methods for fault detection to achieve fast selective protection in DC grids. Although the targeted application in this paper was HVDC, the methods are applicable

MVDC grids are fed by AC-DC or DC-DC converters; some topologies can limit DC-side fault current, while others lose current control if DC voltage collapses. For DC faults, one strategy is AC-side protection: if converters lack DC-side control or blocking capability and no fast DC breakers are installed, converters are blocked and AC-side breakers opened; this is used for point-to-point HVDC links but is not acceptable for multi-node DC grids. Another strategy is selective protection with fast DC circuit breakers in all DC lines, allowing fault isolation before converters are blocked; detection, location, and isolation must be completed in a few milliseconds or less to avoid excessive fault current and converter blocking.

normalized_text: reactive power that the converter to support a connected AC-grid by reactive power. This requirement generally puts a more severe limitation on the neutralization speed since the converter semiconductor switches tend to be the most sensitive part of the system. Studies reviews various methods for fault detection to achieve fast selective protection in DC grids. Although the targeted application in this paper was HVDC, the methods are applicable to MVDC as well. 3) Open-grid protection: Another way of protecting DC grids is the so called open grid protection concept. It aims to address the problem that it may take to long to locate the fault, during which time the fault current will keep rising. Therefore, it stipulates that all DC breakers in the system are opened as soon as a fault is detected. Subsequently, the faulty line is identified and the breakers connecting to the other lines in the system are closed again, and power flow on these is restored. One of the main benefits of the open grid protection strategy is the distribution of the breaker energy absorption requirements over different breakers. This way the MOVs of each circuit breaker may be rated for less energy than would be required with the fully selective protection. The obvious drawback, on the other hand, is that any fault will interrupt the power flow in the entire DC grid. . One of the main benefits of the open grid protection strategy is the distribution of the breaker energy absorption requirements over different breakers. This way the MOVs of each circuit breaker may be rated for less energy than would be required with the fully selective protection. The obvious drawback, on the other hand, is that any fault will interrupt the power flow in the entire DC grid. 4) Protection using current-limiting converters and fast DC switches: A further fault-handling method relies on using converters capable of rapidly blocking or limiting the DC-side current. Notably, some converter topologies that may be used as interfaces to MVDC grids have the ability to rapidly limit or even control fault current (within microseconds). This applies to, for instance, the full-bridge MMC and the Dual Active Bridge (DAB) DC/DC converter. It makes it possible to rapidly reduce the DC currents fed into the grid from the converters in case of a fault. Thereby, the fault can be isolated by fast DC switches rather than DC circuit breakers. This method has the drawback that, for AC/DC conversion, considerably more costly converters need to be used. In the case of modular multiphase converter, the DC current-limiting variety, with full-bridge cells, suffers from much higher semiconductor cost than the conventional MMC with half-bridges. Also the power losses are higher, since the current has to pass through two power semiconductors in each cell. Furthermore, since all converters need to reduce their DC-side current to zero the power flow in the entire grid will cease until the fault has been cleared. A benefit of the method is that DC circuit breakers are not needed, only fast DC switches, which are less expensive to implement.

normalized_text: For the rating and parameter selection of the components of the protection system, there is a need to determine the time evolution of voltages and currents during a fault transient, including its clearing by the protection system. A DC system differs from an AC system in this regard. In an AC system, short-circuit currents can be calculated using steady-state methods, correcting for the voltage at the fault inception to determine the peak instantaneous current. In a DC system, however, the prospective fault current cannot be allowed to reach its limit, since it is usually limited only by the system resistance. The fault current magnitude could disrupt the entire grid and cause damage to components such as converters and DC circuit breakers. As fault currents need to be limited during their rise, the transient evolution of currents and voltages needs to be studied. This is best done by circuit simulators such as Simulink, PLECS or PSCAD. From a detailed circuit simulation, several key results must be derived. Some examples are the following: The peak current through the circuit breaker at the time of commutation of the fault current to the Metal-Oxide Varistor (MOV); The rate of rise of the fault current; The voltage and current stress on different components of the grid during the fault clearing transient; The dissipated energy in the MOV of the circuit breaker; Whether the circumstances make it necessary to block one or several converters, and for how long the blocking has to be maintained; What protection algorithms are useful and will detect faults with sufficient reliability and selectivity. A useful approach is generally to apply the simulated current and voltage waveforms to different protection algorithms to evaluate their performance in the relevant case.

normalized_text: There are several kinds of DC switchgear with different functionality. This report is focusing on defining requirements for MVDC Circuit Breakers, since DC fault current interruption is challenging, and requires the most research and development effort. Still, for completeness, a brief introduction to some different kinds of DC switchgears that may be used in MVDC grids is given in the sections below, see also ref (CIGRE Technical Brochure 683: Technical Requirements and Specifications of State-of-the-Art HVDC Switching Equipment, published in 2017. 3.1.1 Disconnectors: A disconnector (also known as isolator switch) lacks any significant current interruption capability, but needs to withstand the full system voltage and, depending on the application, it may also have making capability, i.e. that it can turn on current, potentially with a latent short-circuit fault present. It is used for isolation and safety purposes, to ensure that a circuit is galvanically separated from a source. DC circuit breakers usually rely on having a MOV arrestor connected in parallel, to absorb magnetic energy during interruption. Thus, there will not be a complete galvanic separation after opening the breaker and a small current will flow though the MOV (less than one per cent of the nominal current) even after the breaker has opened. Therefore, there will generally be a disconnector connected in series, which will need a very limited interruption capability (few amperes). 3.1.2 Earthing Switches and Transfer Switches: In HVDC applications there is also a number of specialized switchgear types used. These may possibly be used also for MVDC, at least at the higher end of the MVDC range. First, earthing switches are used for earthing and short-circuiting a de-energized converter or part of a line during maintenance and during testing. They need to be able to carry current for a limited time, but not continuously. In some cases they need to have making capability. Another category is the transfer switches. Their role is to transfer load current from one circuit to another. In bipolar HVDC systems they are typically used to transfer the load current to and from the metallic return branch, to allow for seamless transfer between bipolar and monopolar operation. Possibly this application will also be relevant and beneficial for MVDC system operation therefor such needs making (turn-on) capability and interruption capability, up to the load current in both cases. 3. . In bipolar HVDC systems they are typically used to transfer the load current to and from the metallic return branch, to allow for seamless transfer between bipolar and monopolar operation. Possibly this application will also be relevant and beneficial for MVDC system operation therefor such needs making (turn-on) capability and interruption capability, up to the load current in both cases. 3. 1.3 DC Circuit Breakers: The task of a Direct Current Circuit Breaker (DCCB) is generally to interrupt DC current. Since no current zero-crossings occur in DC circuits this means that a breaker should be able to provide a counter-voltage of sufficient magnitude to force the current down to zero. The next section will discuss in more detail how this is commonly achieved. A basic DCCB will not achieve galvanic separation after interruption. An MOV, and in some cases other circuitry, will remain connected, permitting a leakage current to flow. A DCCB is therefore in many cases combined with a series-connected disconnector able to interrupt the leakage current and achieve galvanic isolation. The disconnector may be considered part of the DCCB's delivery scope or provided as a separate part. With no further qualifications, a DCCB is assumed to be able to interrupt a fault current. However, there are also load-current breakers whose interruption capability is limited to the normal operating current.

normalized_text: leakage current and achieve galvanic isolation. The disconnector may be considered as part of the delivery scope of the DCCB or be provided as a separate part. With no further qualifications, a DCCB is assumed to be able to interrupt a fault current. However, there are also load current breakers where the interruption capability is limited to the normal operating current. In general, a DCCB will also need making capability, i.e., the ability to turn on in a situation where a current will immediately start to flow. Often it is even required that it should be possible to turn on with a short-circuit fault applied, meaning that a fault current surge will occur instantly. The breaker will then obviously also need to immediately interrupt the current. A common cause of such faults is that a short-circuiting rod has been left in place by mistake after a maintenance event. 3.2 Generic Configuration of a DC Circuit Breaker The interruption of direct current in case of a grid fault differs fundamentally from AC current interruption. Since there are no current zero-crossings it is necessary to activate the fault current down to zero, overcoming the magnetic energy of the grid section between power feeding in and the fault location. (a) an idealized generic arrangement to accomplish this is displayed. It consists of an ideal switch, that can open even if a current flows though it, with an MOV connected in parallel. The MOV is assumed to also have ideal properties, meaning that it conducts no current at all below a certain level, labeled the protection level, and easily conducts current beyond it, thus limiting the voltage at the protection level. In practice, an actual MOV shows a non-ideal characteristic, but for the initial understanding of the DCCB operation this assumption is acceptable. (b) shows the principal waveforms of the current and voltage during a fault current commutation procedure using this arrangement. Initially, the load current is carried by the switch, which provides the normal current path. At the fault inception the current starts rising, and after a short while, the relay time, the IED has detected the fault and the DCCB will be commanded to open. The opening of the ideal switch is then commenced and after the internal current commutation time has passed the commutation to the MOV happens. The sum of the relay time and the internal current commutation time is labelled the neutralization time, t_n. As the current is forced to flow through the MOV the protection level fault and the DCCB will be commanded to open. The opening of the ideal switch is then commenced and after the internal current commutation time has passed the commutation to the MOV happens. The sum of the relay time and the internal current commutation time is labelled the neutralization time, t_n. As the current is forced to flow through the MOV the protection level voltage will appear across it. Provided that this voltage exceeds the grid voltage the line current will be forced down to zero. All modern implementations of fast DCCBs follow this basic paradigm. They differ mostly in terms of the way that the current is transferred from the normally conducting branch to the MOV branch, i.e., the way that the ideal switch is implemented. In section 3.4, a few different types of DCCBs are described, that implement this function in different ways. 3.3 Relationships Between Grid and Breaker Parameter During Fault Transients

A DCCB generally also needs making capability, including turn-on with a short-circuit fault applied. DC fault interruption differs from AC because there are no current zero-crossings; the fault current must be driven to zero. An idealized DCCB uses an ideal switch in parallel with an MOV. The MOV conducts no current below a protection level and conducts above it, limiting voltage at that level. During a fault, current initially flows through the switch; after relay time the IED commands opening, then after internal current commutation time the current transfers to the MOV. Relay time plus internal current commutation time is the neutralization time, t_n. If the MOV protection-level voltage exceeds the grid voltage, line current is forced to zero.

normalized_text: The requirements on the circuit breaker performance depend on among others the configuration of the protected grid, the type of the protected converter and the presence of a current limiting reactor. Moreover, in case of a DCCB, the insulation coordination of the grid and the length of the conductors also impact the design of the energy absorbing branch of the circuit breaker. In the following list, the contribution of these factors to the circuit breaker requirements are detailed. 1. Grid configuration: The configuration of the grid plays a role in whether pole-to-ground faults result in large fault currents or not. In case the pole-to-ground faults do not cause quickly rising fault currents, a single circuit breaker capable of clearing pole-to-pole faults may be sufficient to protect the transmission line. If a pole-to-ground fault results in large fault currents, then both poles of the transmission line should be protected by a dedicated circuit breaker. 2. Converter type: The converter type plays a role in how large the initial fault current amplitudes can be in case of a short circuit occurring electrically close to the converter. More energy stored in the converter DC side capacitor results in larger initial fault current surge peaks and rise times. The MMC converters are more advantageous in this respect as they have their DC side capacitance distributed along the submodules of their converter arms. 3. Current limiting reactor: Depending on the type of circuit breaker used, a current limiting DC reactor needs to be designed into a grid so that it reduces the rate of rise of all types of faults currents in that grid. The reduction of the rate of rise of current should be sufficient so that the circuit breaker is enabled to break before the fault current exceeds its maximum breaking current. The reactor design should also take into account many other technical require-ments (e.g. nominal current, available space for the reactor, basic insulation level, losses, electromagnetic interference with other components etc.). 4. Insulation coordination: The insulation coordination of a grid influences how large the clamping voltage of the DCCB energy absorbing branch can be. This in turn impacts how fast a fault current suppression can be and therefore how much energy the branch should absorb during a single breaking operation. As there are no established standards regarding the insulation coordination in MVDC grids as of the writing of this report (early 2022), setting the nominal system voltage does not automatically determine the clamping voltage of the circuit breaker; it rather yields a range of feasible clamping voltages. 5. Conductor length how much energy the branch should absorb during a single breaking operation. As there are no established standards regarding the insulation coordination in MVDC grids as of the writing of this report (early 2022), setting the nominal system voltage does not automatically determine the clamping voltage of the circuit breaker; it rather yields a range of feasible clamping voltages. 5. Conductor length : During a breaking operation the magnetic energy stored in the grid conductors has to be dissipated in the energy absorbing branch of the DCCB. This magnetic energy depends on the peak fault current amplitude at the beginning of the fault current suppression phase and on the inductance of the protected lines. The latter is proportional to the length of the conductors.

normalized_text: Fault Detection: Depending on the size of the grid, there are several ways in which faults can be detected. Many protection strategies involve relays that measure the impedance of the conductors between the substation and the fault location, check instantaneous currents through them against preset levels, or compare current derivatives to preset values. The information about the fault that is yielded by the protection relays can be used to determine which circuit breakers should operate, and the central control system can issue the trip commands to the circuit breakers. It, however, may take several milliseconds from fault inception till the breaker neutralizes the fault. In DC grids, it is anticipated that there is insufficient time for the circuit breaker to break the fault current. Therefore, it is necessary to design the circuit breaker so that it can autonomously decide whether to interrupt the current through its VI. If the current is evolving in a way that would reach the circuit breaker's maximum breaking current before a higher-level or central control system could detect a fault, relay the trip command, and execute the breaking operation, then the circuit breaker should be able to protect itself. This self-protection can be implemented by measuring the current through the VI for a short time and extrapolating it. Since the maximum breaking current of the circuit breaker and the breaker operation time are known, a control algorithm can decide if tripping the circuit breaker at the given moment is necessary. For the VARC circuit breaker, fault detection takes approximately 200 µs, and breaker operation time is approximately 1 ms. Therefore, a fault neutralization time of 1.2 ms is expected.

normalized_text: The simulations investigate the development of fault current in a medium-voltage DC grid without circuit breaker intervention and determine the required breaker operation speed and line reactor size based on converter capacitor characteristics. A simplified grid model is used, in which converters are represented by an equivalent AC source and a rectifier. Artificial voltage and current sources maintain pre-fault conditions, and additional resistance is included to prevent numerical oscillations. The initial fault current is driven by capacitor discharge, with a pre-fault current of 1 kA assumed. Five fault scenarios are analyzed: 1) Short circuit close to the converter without a line reactor: The fault current rises extremely rapidly, reaching very high values within milliseconds and exceeding the interruption capability of most DC circuit breakers. This demonstrates that additional inductance is required. 2) Short circuit close to the converter with a line reactor: The line reactor significantly slows the rate of current rise, enabling the use of circuit breakers with feasible breaking capacity and operation time. The required inductance depends on system voltage, pre-fault current, and breaker characteristics. 3) Short circuit along the cable with a line reactor: Cable impedance contributes to limiting the fault current rise in addition to the line reactor. Although the current waveform depends on the fault location, the protection requirements remain similar, and a properly rated circuit breaker can protect the system. 4) Short circuit close to the converter with reduced DC capacitance and without a line reactor: Reducing the capacitor size lowers the peak fault current, but the rate of current rise remains too high for safe interruption. A line reactor is still required. 5) Short circuit close to the converter with reduced DC capacitance and a line reactor: Combining reduced capacitance with a line reactor allows for a smaller inductance while still enabling successful fault interruption. However, excessive reduction of inductance is not recommended, as it may compromise protection performance.

normalized_text: Conclusions. This report analyzes several aspects of a prospective MVDC grid design. These aspects play a key role in determining the requirements for the DCCB that is to protect the grid. Section 2 presents the different grid topologies and fault-handling strategies that an MVDC grid is likely to adopt in the future. Section 3: various DC circuit breaker types which may be used in MVDC networks. Section 4 presents a set of generic circuit breaker ratings and parameters to be considered during the design phase of a DCCB. Section 5 reviews the state of the art in relevant standards and technical papers. Sections 6 and 7 detail the requirements for DCCBs imposed by the already-built and commissioned part of the FEN Research Grid in Aachen and by a conceptual 14 kV MVDC system, respectively. Future work, including refinement of the circuit breaker requirements, is expected to be done once DCCBs have been installed in the FEN Research Grid as part of the HYPERRIDE project and they start to provide operational experience. Moreover, the publication of additional technical brochures and papers listed in Section 5 can help identify new directions where research activity may be necessary.

The report states that these aspects determine the requirements for a DCCB that protects the grid.

Sections 6 and 7 detail DCCB requirements imposed by the already-built and commissioned part of the FEN Research Grid in Aachen and by a conceptual 14 kV MVDC system, respectively.

compacted_text: This report analyzes aspects of a prospective MVDC grid design that determine requirements for the DCCB protecting the grid. It covers future MVDC grid topologies and fault-handling strategies, DC circuit breaker types for MVDC networks, generic circuit breaker ratings and parameters for DCCB design, and the state of the art in relevant standards and technical papers. Sections 6 and 7 detail DCCB requirements imposed by the built and commissioned part of the FEN Research Grid in Aachen and by a conceptual 14 kV MVDC system. Future work includes refining circuit breaker requirements after DCCBs are installed in the FEN Research Grid as part of the HYPERRIDE project and operational experience is available; additional technical brochures and papers may identify new research directions.

heading_path: Main > 2. Background > 2.2 Use of communication between controller and breaker
normalized_text: As part of the HYPERRIDE project, Scibreak must deploy two MVDC circuit breakers at the Aachen demo site. These circuit breakers are designed to interrupt line currents in both normal and fault conditions. A characteristic trait of DC systems is the rapid rise in fault currents compared to those in AC systems. Therefore, DC circuit breakers need quick fault detection, decision-making, and current interruption. The requirement for speed is quite stringent; the targeted fault-neutralization time is 1–1.5 ms. The breaker operating time is in the range of 0.8–1.2 ms, leaving only around 0.5 ms for fault detection and communication to the breaker. This type of reaction speed is only necessary for handling extreme fault cases, but even so, those cases require maximum communication latency. The standard family IEC 61850 (which includes the GOOSE protocol, whose use in this project is detailed further at the end of this Section) is a possible choice for adhering to when designing communication platforms for industrial systems. As the protocols in IEC 61850 are Ethernet-based, this imposes a limit on the maximum communication speed between communicating parties due to latency. The relevant standard version applicable at the time of writing this report is IEC 61850-5-2013, which defines the transfer time for a ‘Trip’ signal to be no more than 3 ms. Therefore, it cannot be taken for granted that a trip command issued by a higher-order control device would always reach the circuit breaker faster than this. The requirement for fault-neutralization time, combined with the IEC 61850 standard family's communication speed requirements, implies that the circuit breakers must be autonomous. The converter control computer cannot actively participate in the operation of the MVDC circuit breakers for short-circuit protection. However, the computer could use the data collected by the sensors of the circuit breaker, such as line currents through the breaker or voltage over the VI, as help for monitoring the grid. Moreover, for grid topology changes, the computer could issue open/close orders, resulting in the connection or disconnection of parts of the protected system. These orders would be interpreted by the breaker controller, and different operations would be performed. If the breaker is opened when no or only small currents flow through the Vacuum Interrupter (VI), the Residual Current Switch (RCS) connected in series with the CB (and integrated into the circuit breaker cabinet, which

heading_path: Main > 3. Targeted Safety and Functional Features > 3.1 Basic Smart Grid Functions > 3.1.8 Selective Feeder Protection
normalized_text: As the DC branch is connected to the AC grid, any fault on the DC side will affect the feeder. The protection coordination should detect the fault and disconnect the affected feeder (due to a rapid increase in AC current). In case of multiple feeders to the DC microgrid, the selective protection procedure can maintain the connection to the unaffected feeders and minimize the impact of the fault.
statements: Because the DC branch is connected to the AC grid, any fault on the DC side will affect the feeder.
Selective protection can disconnect only the affected feeder while maintaining connection to unaffected feeders in a DC microgrid with multiple feeders.

In case of multiple feeders to the DC microgrid, the selective protection procedure can maintain the connection to the unaffected feeders and minimize the impact of the fault.

heading_path: Main > 5. Microgrid Control, Protection Coordination, and Grounding Configuration > 5.2 Protection Strategy
normalized_text: The protection strategy in a DC microgrid must account for the main distinguishing feature of DC power distribution: its low line capacitance, which results in very low grid latency. Fault propagation and di/dt values for the short-circuit current are much faster than in the AC grid (1-3 ms vs. 10-50 ms). The protection concepts should implement several features as follows: (1) Sensitivity: detection of a faulty link only if a fault is present (avoid nuisance tripping); (2) Selectivity: correct identification of faulty grid branches; (3) Timing: the total time required to detect a fault and to activate the protection operation must be short enough to prevent equipment damage (4) Reliability: the protection scheme performs correctly by considering main and backup criteria; (5) Seamless operation: after the fault clearance, the remaining part of the system continues to operate normally; 6) Resilience: detection of faults even in case of partial operation mode and selectivity from any operational events. There are several methods than can be combined to avoid damages and expensive repairs to the grid converters and loads:(1) Over-size the converters: If the converters' current capability is comparable to the estimated fault current, a high fault current will not damage the equipment. However, this method requires expensive, bulkier converters and steady-state operation at lower efficiency. (2) Use fuses: This strategy has a low implementation cost but results in longer downtime when a fault occurs. The fuses need to be physically replaced. (3) Use DC circuit breakers: Although breakers are more expensive than fuses, they can have automatic disconnection and reconnection capabilities, allowing minimal downtime, automatic reconfiguration, and remote control. The initial protection configuration proposed for the Terni demonstrator.
statements: DC microgrid protection must account for the low line capacitance of DC power distribution, which results in very low grid latency.
Fault propagation and di/dt values for short-circuit current are much faster in a DC grid than in an AC grid.
The protection concepts should include sensitivity by detecting a faulty link only if a fault is present to avoid nuisance tripping.
The protection concepts should include selectivity by correctly identifying faulty grid branches.
The protection concepts should include timing such that the total time to detect a fault and activate protection is short enough to prevent equipment damage.
The protection concepts should include reliability by considering main and backup criteria for correct operation.
The chunk describes methods to avoid damage and expensive repairs, including oversizing converters, using fuses, and using DC circuit breakers.
compacted_text: DC microgrid protection must account for low line capacitance and very low grid latency; fault propagation and short-circuit di/dt are much faster than in AC grids (1–3 ms vs. 10–50 ms). Protection concepts should provide sensitivity, selectivity, short detection/activation timing to prevent equipment damage, reliability with main and backup criteria, seamless operation after fault clearance, and resilience in partial operation mode. Methods to avoid damage to converters and loads include oversizing converters, using fuses, or using DC circuit breakers. Oversizing requires expensive, bulkier converters and lower steady-state efficiency; fuses have low implementation cost but longer downtime and must be physically replaced; DC circuit breakers are more expensive but allow automatic disconnection/reconnection, minimal downtime, automatic reconfiguration, and remote control.

heading_path: Main > 6. Proposed Converters and Specifications > 6.2 Bidirectional DC/DC Buck/Boost Converter
normalized_text: As an energy buffer, a battery bank is proposed to balance power in use cases with increased penetration of renewable energy sources, such as photovoltaic systems. The battery must be connected to the 700 V DC bus, and power flow is controlled by a bidirectional DC/DC converter for charging and discharging. In the proposed configuration, this converter is selected for a nominal power of 40 kW, with a 400 V low-side (battery) voltage and a 700 V high-side (DC grid) voltage. For the proposed buck-boost converter, the controller should enable closed-loop operation and subsequently integrate into the grid. In grid-connected mode, the converter follows the DC bus voltage and controls the charging or discharging current of the battery. The battery voltage will vary, depending on the battery state of charge. The reference for the current controller is obtained from the corresponding power set-point calculated by the energy management system. In hybrid-grid islanded mode, the converter operates in voltage-controlled mode, setting the main DC bus voltage to a reference. For the demonstrator, a Zekalabs RedPrime DC/DC converter was proposed. This converter is also bidirectional, as required, with forced-air cooling. Communication with the converter can be via several interfaces: MODBUS RTU over RS485, MODBUS TCP/IP, CANBUS, and PROFIBUS. The smart EV charger will be provided by EMOT, the responsible partner for electric mobility in the Italian pilot site; EMOT will redesign one of its commercial fast EV chargers to be connected to the 700 V DC bus. In practice, this upgrade will be implemented by replacing the AC/DC converter typically used in a DC charger with a bidirectional buck/boost DC/DC converter. This device should have a similar operating regime to the battery converter. While charging, the EV Battery Management System (BMS) monitors the battery temperature and limits the charging current to prevent unsafe conditions. The maximum charging power of the proposed EV charger is 40 kW. If the EV BMS allows higher charging powers (above the 40 kW limit), the converter in the EV charger will switch to the current control mode, regulating the current from the DC bus, such that the power does not exceed the maximum value. In V2G mode, the converter regulates the current supplied to the DC bus using a power-flow optimization algorithm, while adjusting the input current from the battery to match the discharge power with the power transferred to the the converter in the EV charger will switch to the current control mode, regulating the current from the DC bus, such that the power does not exceed the maximum value. In V2G mode, the converter regulates the current supplied to the DC bus using a power-flow optimization algorithm, while adjusting the input current from the battery to match the discharge power with the power transferred to the DC grid. Again, the battery voltage will be variable, depending on the state of charge.
