heading_path: Main > 3. LITERATURE REVIEW > 3.1 Principal “Green” Interventions across Port Operational Areas
normalized_text: Hinterland Connection Activities—Key Interventions: Hinterland connectivity focuses on freight movements between ports and inland logistics hubs. Key interventions include: (i) The deployment of electric and hydrogen-powered heavy trucks reduces emissions in short-haul logistics and complies with tightening EU emissions standards. (ii) The emergence of low-carbon logistics corridors enhances sustainability along entire supply chains, with supporting infrastructure including charging and hydrogen refuelling stations critical for wide adoption. (iii) Digital freight platforms further optimise routing and scheduling, cutting emissions and increasing operational efficiency. Electrification has become a transformative element for port operations, redefining equipment, fleets, and supporting systems. Combined with electricity from RES, this shift supports the transition away from fossil fuels, reduces the carbon footprint, and aligns ports with regulatory and policy trends. The widespread adoption of SSE-OPS standards out as a key intervention, allowing ships to connect to shore power, eliminating auxiliary engine use. It is mandated under the EU’s AFIR and FuelEU Maritime Regulation for TEN-T core ports by 2030. Financial mechanisms, including the CEF programme, have accelerated its deployment. As electrification technologies become more embedded in global port practices, literature and port case studies consistently confirm their role in driving sustainability and operational innovation. The measures discussed, summarised, capture the primary categories of electrification actions that are both technologically relevant and policy-driven. These domains reflect a convergence of academic, regulatory, and industry focus, highlighting where real-world implementation is occurring and where future interventions can be most impactful. The electrification of Ground Support Vehicles (GSV), including cargo handling equipment, stackers, and forklifts, and the port-owned vessels has been widely studied as a critical component of electrification in ports. Numerous studies have highlighted the shift from conventional to electric propulsion as a critical step toward reducing emissions and enhancing efficiency [8–10]. In parallel, electric-powered cranes such as RTGs, stacking cranes, and ship-to-shore gantry cranes have been thoroughly examined due to their capacity to lower fuel use and maintenance costs while supporting broader environmental goals. Retrofitting the equipment has also received considerable attention, offering a cost-effective alternative to full replacement. However, this approach presents technical challenges related to integration with existing systems. The installation of charging hubs for external electric trucks reflects growing interest in greening hinterland connectivity, with studies focusing on cost-effectiveness, design, and operational reliability. Electrification of supporting broader environmental goals. Retrofitting the equipment has also received considerable attention, offering a cost-effective alternative to full replacement. However, this approach presents technical challenges related to integration with existing systems. The installation of charging hubs for external electric trucks reflects growing interest in greening hinterland connectivity, with studies focusing on cost-effectiveness, design, and operational reliability. Electrification of auxiliary port vessels, including support craft and tugs, is another emerging research focus. Transitions to hybrid-electric, fully electric, or alternative fuels offer substantial benefits in reducing emissions and noise. Lastly, investments in SSE-OPS, whether powered by RES or traditional grids, have been extensively analysed. While grid-based systems offer scalability, RES-integrated shore power is consistently identified as the most environmentally beneficial solution due to its potential for near-zero emissions.

heading_path: Main > 3. LITERATURE REVIEW > 3.2 Areas of PESTLE Framework > General
normalized_text: The PESTLE framework is widely recognised as an effective tool for strategic decision-making in complex systems with external influences, such as ports. It enables stakeholders to systematically identify, prioritise, and address factors affecting electrification while aligning with long-term sustainability objectives. This paper adopts PESTLE due to its capacity to capture the multifaceted external challenges and opportunities facing port electrification and its alignment with contemporary academic and industry practice. In dynamic contexts marked by regulatory evolution, financial constraints, and technological change, PESTLE offers actionable insights for strategic planning and policy formulation. Its relevance is underscored by recent applications in port-related research. Studies have integrated PESTLE with the Balanced Scorecard and smart port principles to address port inefficiencies, environmental challenges, and technological gaps. Other work has applied PESTLE within a technology roadmap to identify key drivers of cleaner propulsion adoption. Similarly, PESTLE combined with SWOT has been used to examine the deployment of energy management systems in North-European ports. The economic dimension dominates, highlighting investment concerns, funding, and cost-benefit evaluations. Technological factors reflect infrastructure readiness and innovation. Social elements focus on workforce skills, public perception, and stakeholder support. Legal and environmental dimensions reflect regulatory pressures and sustainability mandates, while political factors relate to governance and public policy. Overall, this distribution underscores the multidimensional nature of port electrification and the value of PESTLE in structuring such analyses. -benefit evaluations. Technological factors reflect infrastructure readiness and innovation. Social elements focus on workforce skills, public perception, and stakeholder support. Legal and environmental dimensions reflect regulatory pressures and sustainability mandates, while political factors relate to governance and public policy. Overall, this distribution underscores the multidimensional nature of port electrification and the value of PESTLE in structuring such analyses. Political Determinants of Port Electrification: Political factors play a critical role in shaping port electrification, acting both as enablers and constraints. On the supportive side, governments offer subsidies, grants, and tax incentives to offset the high upfront costs of adopting electrified technologies such as GSVs, electric cranes, and support vessels. Within the EU, funding has significantly advanced renewable energy projects, particularly shore power facilities, reducing ports’ dependence on conventional fuels. The Energy Taxation Directive permits Member States to exempt electricity supplied to berthed vessels from taxation, promoting the uptake of shore-side electricity systems as a cleaner alternative to onboard diesel generators. Major regulatory instruments require shore-side electricity installation in core ports by 2030, further accelerating electrification. Similarly, the introduction of more rigorous emissions standards for heavy-duty vehicles has provided additional incentives for the development of charging infrastructure for electric trucks. Political support also encourages collaboration across ports, governments, and industries, helping to standardise solutions and expand implementation. Broader political commitments, such as long-term sustainability strategies, further support investments in electrification technologies. Nevertheless, several political obstacles remain. Resistance from established organisations and shipping companies reluctant to embrace disruptive changes, along with ambiguities in regulatory frameworks—such as unclear responsibilities for infrastructure ownership and operational costs—impede progress. Bureaucratic complexity poses another barrier, as large-scale projects like shore power facilities often require approvals from multiple authorities, leading to extended timelines. Variations in national regulations also complicate implementation by requiring adaptation to diverse standards. Despite these challenges, aligning political will with economic and technical strategies remains essential to accelerate the transition toward sustainable port operations.

heading_path: Main > 3. LITERATURE REVIEW > 3.2 Areas of PESTLE Framework > General
normalized_text: —impede progress. Bureaucratic complexity poses another barrier, as large-scale projects like shore power facilities often require approvals from multiple authorities, leading to extended timelines. Variations in national regulations also complicate implementation by requiring adaptation to diverse standards. Despite these challenges, aligning political will with economic and technical strategies remains essential to accelerate the transition toward sustainable port operations. Economic Determinants of Port Electrification: The electrification of ports offers transformative benefits in terms of operational efficiency, sustainability, and long-term economic performance. A key enabler of this transition is the mobilisation of public and private financing mechanisms, including partnerships between public authorities and original equipment manufacturers, which help offset high upfront costs. Strategic collaboration between terminal operators and manufacturers enhances technical compatibility and cost efficiency. Electric cargo-handling equipment can offer a lower total cost of operation than diesel alternatives, reducing operating expenses. Ports that support electric truck infrastructure can also provide incentives such as reduced parking fees, increasing their operational attractiveness. Funding mechanisms support the implementation of shore-side electricity systems, improving financial viability. Electrification reduces exposure to fossil fuel price volatility and provides greater long-term cost certainty. Ports adopting renewable energy-based shore power or electrified equipment can enhance energy resilience and mitigate price fluctuations. In addition, electrification initiatives can contribute to productivity gains and job creation, particularly where new infrastructure is deployed. Retrofitting existing machinery, although technically challenging, can be more cost-effective and require less retraining and infrastructure overhaul. Despite these benefits, economic barriers remain significant. High initial capital investment for equipment acquisition, retrofitting, charging infrastructure, and shore-side electricity facilities is a major hurdle, particularly for smaller ports with limited financing capacity. Electric cranes, for example, are often more expensive than diesel-powered alternatives. Uncertainty around return on investment—driven by evolving technologies, energy price fluctuations, and unclear usage levels—makes ports cautious about committing to large-scale investments. Operational electricity costs, especially for grid-based systems, can also reduce anticipated savings. Some ports have mitigated these risks through tax exemptions and strategic electricity purchasing, achieving cost parity with traditional fuel-based operations, although such approaches often require significant upfront investment. Electrifying port trucking fleets is further constrained by limited fast-charging infrastructure, range limitations, and high vehicle costs.

Electrifying port trucking fleets is further constrained by limited fast-charging infrastructure, range limitations, and high vehicle costs. Social Determinants of Port Electrification: The social dimension significantly influences the adoption of port electrification initiatives, acting as both a driver and a barrier. Public demand for cleaner and more sustainable operations plays a crucial role in shaping port decisions. Electrification measures such as electric cranes, shore-side electricity systems, and GSVs enhance air quality and reduce diesel emissions, contributing to improved public health and better working conditions for port personnel. These improvements support stronger community relations and enhance the social licence to operate, particularly when noise and pollution are reduced through technologies such as shore-side electricity. Moreover, environmentally conscious clients and consumers increasingly expect sustainable practices, strengthening the reputation of electrified ports as modern and responsible logistics hubs. Workforce development is another key social benefit. Training programmes associated with electrification projects support the upskilling of workers to operate and maintain advanced systems, reinforcing internal capacity and local employment. These investments also improve safety and reduce reliance on external technical expertise. In some ports, the social impact of emissions has been quantified, with health-related external costs representing a substantial economic burden, particularly those associated with particulate matter. Despite these benefits, social resistance remains a barrier. Established stakeholders, particularly large shipping companies and port operators, may be hesitant to overhaul existing systems due to cost and operational complexities. Adapting vessels and infrastructure to accommodate shore-side electricity systems requires alignment with evolving technical standards, presenting both logistical and financial challenges. Ports are responding with incentives such as differentiated port dues to encourage adoption, while also fostering collaboration with shipping companies to ensure technical compatibility and operational alignment. Ultimately, while social drivers such as improved health outcomes, local employment, and public support provide a compelling case for electrification, challenges such as resistance to change and skill shortages require proactive management. Addressing these barriers will require sustained stakeholder engagement, effective communication strategies, and targeted workforce training to ensure a smooth and inclusive transition to electrified port operations.

normalized_text: challenges such as resistance to change and skill shortages require proactive management. Addressing these barriers will require sustained stakeholder engagement, effective communication strategies, and targeted workforce training to ensure a smooth and inclusive transition to electrified port operations. Technological Determinants of Port Electrification: The technological dimension plays a vital role in both facilitating and constraining port electrification. Key enablers include recent advancements in energy storage, charging technologies, and automation. Developments such as ultra-fast charging, battery swapping systems, and wireless charging technologies minimise downtime and enhance the efficiency of GSVs and electric cranes. Retrofitting has become more feasible due to improvements in hybrid systems and battery technologies, enabling the continued use of existing equipment with reduced emissions. For port vessels, the deployment of advanced batteries and hybrid propulsion systems—combining electric and conventional engines—enables flexible and efficient operations. Fully electric systems are increasingly used for low-demand tasks, while hybrid configurations are better suited to high-demand operations such as towing and dredging. Similarly, electric cranes improve productivity and reduce fuel dependency, making them scalable across ports of varying sizes. The integration of renewable energy sources into shore power facilities, such as solar and wind, enables near-zero-emission operations and strengthens overall port sustainability. Smart grid technologies and energy management systems are also essential for balancing demand, particularly at electric truck charging hubs. Nonetheless, several technological barriers persist. A major challenge is the lack of standardisation across equipment types and manufacturers, which complicates interoperability. Differences in battery specifications, charging systems, and power requirements for GSVs and cranes create additional complexity. Battery management also presents a significant challenge, as improper charging practices can substantially reduce battery lifespan. Addressing this requires targeted workforce training to ensure proper maintenance and handling of batteries, which are among the most costly components of electrified equipment. The integration of renewable energy sources into shore power systems further increases technical complexity, requiring advanced grid management, hybrid system configurations, and robust energy storage solutions to maintain a reliable power supply. Grid capacity constraints during peak demand periods present another significant barrier, particularly as electrification expands. Electrifying external truck fleets also requires heavy-duty charging infrastructure, which can place additional strain on existing grid systems. In addition, battery weight can affect equipment performance and increase energy consumption.In conclusion, while technological advancements provide significant opportunities for decarbonisation, overcoming these constraints will require coordinated investment, the development of international standards, and close collaboration among stakeholders to ensure scalability and operational effectiveness.

Legal Determinants of Port Electrification: The legal dimension significantly shapes the implementation of port electrification, presenting both enabling and constraining factors. A primary driver is the European Union’s regulatory focus on emissions reduction, as outlined in key policy frameworks. Port operations are subject to emissions regulations governing fuel quality and air pollution, which promote the transition to cleaner energy alternatives. These frameworks require Member States to develop national emissions inventories and implement air pollution control strategies, including port-related sustainability initiatives. Emission regulations also extend to port fleets, with standards for heavy-duty vehicles encouraging the adoption of low-emission technologies within port operations. More recent regulatory developments addressing renewable fuels and mandating the use of shore-side electricity systems for berthed vessels have further strengthened the legal case for electrification. These measures not only compel ports to reduce emissions—thereby supporting the electrification of cranes and truck infrastructure—but also reinforce compliance with health and safety standards. Adjustments in national legislation have also contributed to enabling electrification. For example, removing regulatory barriers that classify ports as energy suppliers when providing electricity improves the business case for shore power and facilitates the integration of renewable energy sources. Fiscal measures, such as tax exemptions on electricity supplied to vessels, further reduce the cost differential between electrified solutions and conventional fuels.However, legal uncertainties remain a significant barrier. Ambiguity surrounding ownership and operational responsibility for infrastructure, particularly grid connections, can delay project implementation. Complex permitting procedures also slow down investments in charging infrastructure and retrofitting projects. In some cases, regulatory frameworks have imposed disproportionate administrative burdens on operators, although subsequent reforms have helped align shore-side electricity systems with broader electrification infrastructure, thereby easing implementation. Additionally, integrating renewable energy into shore power systems presents legal challenges related to grid balancing and supply continuity. Contractual complexities with logistics operators and energy providers may also arise when deploying electric cranes and charging infrastructure. Overall, addressing legal uncertainties and harmonising regulatory frameworks across jurisdictions are essential to enable large-scale port electrification.

Environmental Determinants of Port Electrification: A core environmental driver of port electrification is the substantial reduction in harmful emissions, including greenhouse gases, sulphur oxides, nitrogen oxides, and particulate matter, achieved through the adoption of electric-powered cranes, GSVs, and shore power systems. These measures significantly improve air quality and public health in surrounding communities. Shore power facilities also reduce noise pollution by allowing vessels to switch off auxiliary engines while at berth. Additional benefits include reduced reliance on fossil fuels and the potential integration of energy recovery systems, further enhancing overall sustainability. Despite these advantages, environmental challenges persist. The production, use, and disposal of batteries required for electrified equipment involve resource-intensive processes with environmental implications. Managing battery waste and ensuring sustainable recycling remain complex issues. Furthermore, the integration of renewable energy sources into shore power systems introduces variability in energy generation, which can affect supply reliability. Infrastructure development, including retrofitting and the installation of charging hubs, may also have local environmental impacts, particularly in terms of land use and ecosystem disruption during construction. While electrification offers significant environmental benefits, its long-term sustainability depends on effectively managing these associated challenges.

heading_path: Main > 4. RESULTS > General
normalized_text: Political Drivers and Barriers: Political factors both enable and constrain port electrification as highlighted in Table 1. A key barrier is the reluctance of shipowners to adopt shore-side electricity systems, often due to retrofitting costs and operational concerns. Ports also face uncertainty arising from fluctuating energy policies and tariffs, while political instability, inconsistent funding mechanisms, and unclear regulatory enforcement further deter investment. Concerns related to emerging technologies—such as reliability and the need for workforce retraining—also contribute to stakeholder hesitation. Conversely, several political drivers support progress. Subsidies and tax incentives help reduce the initial costs of electrified vehicles and equipment. Tax exemptions on electricity improve the economic viability of shore-side electricity systems. Emission regulations encourage the development of charging infrastructure, while legislative reforms that allow ports to supply electricity without being classified as utility providers strengthen business models. In addition, regional cooperation supports standardisation efforts and reduces implementation risks, creating a more favourable policy environment for electrification. Economic Drivers and Barriers: High upfront costs for electrified equipment, grid upgrades, and supporting infrastructure, especially in space-constrained urban ports remain major economic barriers to port electrification (Table 2). normalized_text: These investments often come with uncertain returns, while reliance on grid electricity raises concerns over long-term operating costs and price volatility. Additional expenses include energy storage and limited flexibility compared to conventional fuels. Nonetheless, several incentives enhance feasibility. EU funding and OEM partnerships can ease initial capital burdens. Over time, battery-electric cargo-handling equipment proves more cost-effective than diesel alternatives, with reduced maintenance and energy costs. Operational benefits, such as exemptions from parking fees and unrestricted truck park access, further support adoption. Ports that invest early may also avoid future emissions-related penalties, benefiting from regulatory alignment. In this context, electrification is not only an environmental commitment but also a strategic economic decision for ports seeking resilience, cost predictability, and long-term competitiveness. normalized_text: A key barrier is the reluctance of stakeholders to adopt new systems due to concerns about operational disruptions and unfamiliar procedures. This is often compounded by limited awareness of the environmental, health, and operational benefits of electrification. Skill shortages in managing electrified systems further hinder progress, especially in operating electric trucks, which also require coordination with new stakeholders such as utility and permitting agencies. Despite these challenges, strong societal support for emission reduction drives momentum for electrification. Healthier air quality, reduced noise, and better working conditions foster community and workforce support. The transition also enables workforce upskilling and increased operational resilience. Partnerships can facilitate smoother retrofitting, while electric truck charging during rest periods boosts logistics efficiency. Addressing concerns and promoting training is key to overcoming social barriers and ensuring sustainable implementation. Battery-powered machinery increases demand on local grids, risking power outages if grid capacity is insufficient. A lack of standardisation in battery sizes and charging systems among manufacturers leads to compatibility issues, complicating maintenance and procurement. Additionally, the increased weight of batteries raises energy consumption and challenges performance, while improper charging shortens battery life, requiring skilled personnel for optimal use. SSE-OPS systems demand advanced energy management, storage solutions, and hybrid designs to ensure stable supply. Compatibility challenges with ship systems and integration with renewable energy further complicate implementation. Retrofitting existing terminals is more complex than equipping new ones, especially in space-constrained ports. Despite these hurdles, advancements such as ultra-fast charging, battery swapping, and wireless systems reduce downtime. Standardised technologies and smart energy management enable efficient charging and reduce retraining needs. Electric cranes and battery-electric GSVs offer quieter, cleaner operation. Fast-charging systems and scalable electric RTGs support wider adoption. SSE-OPS benefits from standard connections, and future-proofing through technical standards enhances investment viability, supporting smoother transitions to electrified port operations.

heading_path: Main > 5. DISCUSSION > General
normalized_text: The analysis indicates that the electrification transition is driven by a combination of factors, including strong political and legal mandates, decreasing equipment costs, advancements in charging and grid technologies, and increasing societal and environmental pressures. However, significant barriers remain, including high upfront investment requirements, technological limitations related to standardisation and grid integration, volatile energy costs, and regulatory ambiguities. The transition process also reveals a gap between ambition and capacity. Policy targets can exceed the practical capabilities of ports, particularly those with limited financial or technical resources. Factors such as connection delays, shortages of internal expertise, and budgetary constraints can hinder implementation. Recognising this discrepancy is essential, as effective transition pathways require tailored sequencing, adequate financing, and institutional support to align policy ambitions with local capacities. The evidence suggests a two-speed transition. Electric-powered cranes and GSVs are emerging as near-term solutions due to declining equipment costs, established supplier ecosystems, and predictable operational cycles, which reduce both total cost of ownership and investment risk. In contrast, shore-side electricity systems, while central to achieving near-zero emissions and increasingly mandated, depend on distribution network upgrades, available grid capacity, and viable revenue models. As a result, their widespread deployment is likely to occur over a medium- to long-term horizon. This pattern aligns with findings that assets with stable load profiles and limited site dependencies are adopted earlier, whereas technologies that depend heavily on grid integration require broader system readiness and greater financial resources. Political and legal drivers that support electrification also create a hierarchy of measures based on feasibility. Where technological readiness and operational competence are high, regulatory clarity more easily translates into procurement decisions. Conversely, where integration risks depend on external factors such as network capacity, tariff structures, and vessel traffic patterns, investment tends to be slower and more sensitive to utilisation levels and energy price volatility. This review adopts a multifaceted perspective on electrification by distinguishing between different measures based on their adoption mechanisms and constraints. This approach helps explain observed sequencing patterns. It also reframes shore-side electricity systems primarily as challenges related to grid capacity and financing, rather than purely technological readiness. This distinction has important implications for policy design and support mechanisms.

heading_path: Main > 5. DISCUSSION > General

This review adopts a multifaceted perspective on electrification by distinguishing between different measures based on their adoption mechanisms and constraints. This approach helps explain observed sequencing patterns. It also reframes shore-side electricity systems primarily as challenges related to grid capacity and financing, rather than purely technological readiness. This distinction has important implications for policy design and support mechanisms. The following priorities are identified for policy and port practice: - In the short term, investment should prioritise electric cranes and GSVs to achieve rapid emissions reductions and operational savings. - In the medium to long term, regulatory mandates for electricity provision should be accompanied by grid capacity upgrades, capacity reservation mechanisms, and predictable pricing structures. - Financing instruments should be complemented by mechanisms that ensure sufficient utilisation of electrification infrastructure. - Strategic sequencing should be supported through the development of roadmaps that prioritise assets capable of delivering immediate benefits, while grid infrastructure evolves in parallel. - Investment appraisals should incorporate scenarios for utilisation and energy price variability prior to final decisions on shore-side electricity systems, ensuring effective risk management. This multidimensional analysis contributes to the literature by moving beyond single-factor explanations and providing a structured prioritisation of electrification measures. It offers practical insights for policymakers and port authorities seeking to balance short-term feasibility with long-term strategic objectives. A logical next step is to incorporate these mechanisms into a system dynamics model, enabling scenario analysis of grid readiness, energy prices, and vessel traffic patterns, and assessing how policy instruments such as subsidies, tariff structures, and utilisation guarantees influence the timing and scale of electrification.

heading_path: Main > 6. CONCLUSIONS > General
normalized_text: The present paper makes three distinct contributions to the field. First, it demonstrates that PESTLE analysis, when combined with systematic evidence synthesis, can be used not only to identify external factors but also to translate them into prioritised electrification measures for ports. A review of the literature, supported by two European case studies, shows that progress follows a two-speed pattern. Measures embedded in mature operations and supported by established supplier ecosystems, such as the electrification of equipment, are implemented earlier. In contrast, shore-side electricity systems scale only when regulatory frameworks allow electricity provision, grid capacity is secured, and pricing structures are predictable. The analysis reframes shore-side electricity systems primarily as challenges related to grid capacity and financing, rather than limitations in technological readiness. It distinguishes between binding conditions—such as connection lead times, capacity allocation, tariff design, and utilisation risk—and enabling conditions, including regulatory clarity and operational familiarity. This distinction provides a practical framework for aligning electrification strategies with local constraints. In practice, ports can achieve near-term gains by prioritising the electrification of equipment where operational cycles are stable. At the same time, the deployment of shore-side electricity systems should be integrated into financed grid development programmes that include capacity reservation mechanisms and cost-reflective tariffs. Regulators can support this transition by aligning policy mandates with funded network upgrades and establishing clear legal frameworks for electricity provision. This approach enables ports to realise immediate operational and environmental benefits while addressing longer-term infrastructure requirements. It is recommended that future research build on this framework in two complementary directions. First, dynamic modelling and scenario-based analysis should be used to evaluate investment pathways, explore interactions between electrification measures, and assess the resilience of strategies under varying political and economic conditions. Second, a dedicated systematic review following a structured protocol should be conducted to extend the current findings. This would enable the quantification of identified drivers and barriers and support the translation of qualitative evidence into measurable indicators.

heading_path: Main > Abstract > General
normalized_text: This paper examines the current state of Romanian seaports from the perspective of their transformation into smart ports, using the SWOT model to identify the most suitable strategies. It highlights the strengths and weaknesses of existing infrastructure, as well as the opportunities and risks, to outline coherent and sustainable courses of action for future development. A SWOT analysis was conducted based on information collected from a questionnaire sent to members of the maritime port authority, directors, and staff from various departments of the analyzed ports, as well as direct interviews with experts from the three ports. This analysis served as the foundation for developing strategies aimed at accelerating digitization, improving operational efficiency, and reducing environmental impact. The identified strategies were subsequently ranked using the AHP method. The weights assigned to the ten strategies emphasize the relative importance and systemic influence of each one on the process of ports transforming into smart entities. This study makes a significant contribution to the emerging literature on the transformation of Romanian seaports into “smart ports” by approaching this process through the lens of sustainable port development.

The analysis supported strategies to accelerate digitization, improve operational efficiency, and reduce environmental impact. The ten strategies were ranked using the AHP method, and the assigned weights reflect their relative importance and systemic influence on port transformation into smart entities.

heading_path: Main > 1. Introduction > General
normalized_text: Seaports are essential logistical hubs for international trade, and their ability to adapt to economic and technological changes directly influences their regional competitiveness. The transformation of Romanian seaports into modern, competitive, and sustainable infrastructures depends largely on the degree of automation and digitalization they can achieve. In a global context marked by the competition for logistics efficiency and the pressure of the green transition, the adoption of smart technologies becomes an essential condition for the integration of Romanian ports, especially the port of Constanta, into European and international logistics networks. A first step for the ports enhancement is the implementation of “Internet of Things” (IoT) technologies and smart sensors, which allow for the collection and analysis of realtime data on maritime traffic, equipment status, weather conditions, or emission levels. This information contributes to predictive monitoring of infrastructure and reduces the risk of failures or delays. In parallel, port robotics and autonomous equipment, from automated cranes to automated guided vehicles, increase the safety and efficiency of container handling operations, reducing costs and operating time. Also, new AI technologies ensure the base for Romanian ports to enhance the transport process by large logistic database numerical analyses, providing more efficient operational resources for better cargo operations. Geolocation systems (GIS) can be added to the AI systems, making it possible to track ships’ operations in real-time, providing an enhanced intermodal connectivity for all the transport ways. To coordinate all the partners involved, “smart ports” use integrated digital platforms, which simplify the exchange of data between operators, customs authorities, and transport companies, reducing bureaucracy and human errors. At the same time, interlinked technologies guarantee the transparency and security of logistics transactions, while advanced cybersecurity solutions protect the digital port infrastructure against cyberattacks. To reduce the carbon pollution of Romanian ports, the use of onshore power systems, smart electrical grids, and solar panels is recommended. Better training of the transport operations staff by virtual reality can enhance the port’s activities. The integration of new technologies enables Romanian seaports to transition from traditional workflows to smarter systems capable of meeting modern global trade demands. As a national strategy, this shift aims to ensure transportation economic security and competitiveness in the Black Sea region. The maritime port sector in Romania includes the Port of Constanta, the Midia port area, and the Mangalia port area, with the infrastructure being part of the state domain and under a single authority, the national company “Administration of Maritime Ports” S.A. normalized_text: regulation serve as a maritime port authority, together with the “Romanian Naval Authority” (ANR), both operating as part of the “National Transport and Infrastructure Ministry”, and sharing common “smart port” strategies for all entities involved, including MPAC, Constanta, Midia, and Mangalia. These ports play a key role in connecting Central and Eastern Europe to global maritime regions, acting as transit hubs for many cargoes, passengers, and related services. The growing demands of global transportation, driven by digitalization and sustainability, require faster development of infrastructure and operational processes. The “smart port” concept developed as a response to the demand for logistics workflow optimization by applying modern digital technologies. The implementation of advanced technologies in the Romanian ports is very limited, and the differences with European ports are becoming more significant. The digital enhancement has become an essential condition for maintaining a relevant position in the global transport market. The Constanta port is the largest and most complex port in Romania, having significant challenges due to increasing traffic volume, the need for more efficient terminal management, and integration with multimodal infrastructure. The Midia and Mangalia ports are mainly specialized in industries such as energy, shipbuilding, and bulk cargo transport, requiring smart solutions for environmental monitoring, operational enhancement, and industrial activities. The transformation of the MPAC port members can lead to significant benefits, such as reducing operating times, increasing security and operations traceability, reducing environmental impact, and strengthening Romania’s position as a modern regional hub, creating support for innovation, efficient investments, and modern logistics operations. Due to the major challenges faced by Romanian seaports, this paper aims to analyze how these ports can be developed as smart ports by adopting the most relevant strategies. The originality of this study lies in the fact that, for the Romanian transport sector, it is one of the first studies to systematically address the formulation and prioritization of smart seaport development strategies. Unlike existing studies dedicated to ports in Eastern Europe or the Black Sea region, this research is not limited to identifying strategic directions but explicitly analyzes the synergistic effects between the defined and prioritized strategies, as well as the interdependencies between them. In addition, the study proposes a roadmap for the phased and integrated implementation of strategies, built on the basis of the weights resulting from the application of the AHP method, the dependency relationships between strategies, and their strategic impact. Given that the field of smart port development in Romania is in an emerging phase, this strategic analysis contributes , as well as the interdependencies between them. In addition, the study proposes a roadmap for the phased and integrated implementation of strategies, built on the basis of the weights resulting from the application of the AHP method, the dependency relationships between strategies, and their strategic impact. Given that the field of smart port development in Romania is in an emerging phase, this strategic analysis contributes directly to the expansion of the specialized literature and the development of knowledge, while also providing a relevant decision making framework for those involved in the port transformation process. In this regard, the following research questions are formulated: • How are the defined strategies ranked, according to their importance, for the development of Romanian seaports as smart ports? • What is the level of implementation and the prospects of these strategies identified in Romanian seaports? By analyzing empirical data, this research aims to provide a clear perspective on how these ports can integrate these strategies to enhance competitiveness, resilience, and operational efficiency.

heading_path: Main > 2. Literature Review > General
normalized_text: In today’s global context, characterized by accelerated digitization, economic pressures, and increasingly stringent environmental protection requirements, seaports are undergoing a complex transformation toward smart and sustainable operating models. The “smart port” concept integrates advanced technologies for optimization, increasing energy efficiency, and improving security. This transition opens up numerous opportunities for reducing costs, increasing competitiveness, and mitigating environmental impact, but it also poses significant challenges, such as the need for substantial investment, adaptation of existing infrastructure, interoperability of systems, and ensuring cybersecurity. The successful implementation of this concept depends on the existence of a coherent institutional framework, internationally compatible technical standards, and sustained investment in infrastructure and human capital development. Consequently, the smart port is not just a technological innovation, but a new paradigm of sustainable development that redefines the role of ports in the international economy and modern maritime logistics chains. 2.1. Opportunities and Challenges of the Transition of Ports: Towards Efficiency, Operational Security, and Sustainability The development of “smart ports” is accompanied by a significant increase in interdisciplinary studies, focusing on the implementation of “smart technologies”, energy efficiency, resource management, digital transformation, and strengthening the sustainability and resilience of maritime logistics chains. The implementation of “smart technologies” in ports means integrating advanced digital solutions and automated systems that improve the operation, sustainability, and safety of port activities to create more efficient, safer, faster, and more environmentally friendly ports, transforming them into modern, competitive, and sustainable logistics hubs. Thus, studies offer different approaches to the implementation of “smart technologies” in ports. IoT technologies have a relevant role for the maritime industry, which refer to how these technologies transform the way port and shipping operations are monitored, controlled, and optimized. By integrating smart sensors into equipment, containers, ships, and port infrastructure, IoT enables continuous real-time data collection on cargo status, ship position, equipment performance, and environmental conditions. This information facilitates rapid decision-making, prevents breakdowns through predictive maintenance, and increases operational safety. In ports, IoT helps optimize logistics flows, reduce congestion, and automate cargo storage and handling processes. At the ship level, the technology enables the monitoring of fuel consumption, emissions, and propulsion efficiency, thus supporting sustainability and international regulatory complianc objectives. Overall, IoT is becoming a key element in the digitalization of the maritime industry, ensuring a higher level of transparency, efficiency, and safety. Logistics services in ports are increasingly benefiting from new digital technologies that enable the efficient coordination of cargo, information, and transport flows. The implementation of smart solutions, such as digital supply chain management platforms, advanced IoT monitoring systems, artificial intelligence for route optimization, and automation of handling processes, significantly increases the transparency and speed of logistics operations. These services are reinforced by ongoing investments in modern port infrastructure, including automated terminals, high-capacity communication networks (such as 5G), smart storage systems, and sustainable energy facilities. Ports are thus becoming integrated logistics hubs, capable of providing fast connections between maritime, rail, and road transport, reducing transit times, operational costs, and environmental impact, while improving the competitiveness of the entire logistics chain. Another important component for “smart ports” is the implementation of OPSs, which are an essential measure for reducing emissions and increasing the sustainability of maritime operations. normalized_text: s, which are an essential measure for reducing emissions and increasing the sustainability of maritime operations. By connecting ships to the shore-side electricity grid while docked, OPS allows on -board diesel generators to be shut down, significantly reducing air pollution, noise, and vibrations in port areas. A particularly relevant area of research concerns the implementation of climate-resilient technologies in ports, which is an essential step towards increasing the resilience of maritime infrastructure and maintaining safe trade flows. This includes solutions such as advanced sea level and storm monitoring systems, smart port infrastructure capable of withstanding extreme weather events, and the digitization of operations to optimize energy consumption and reduce emissions. The use of renewable energy sources, such as photovoltaic panels and wind turbines, also contributes to reducing dependence on fossil fuels. By adopting these technologies, ports can respond more effectively to climate risks, ensuring continuity of operations and protecting both the environment and the local economy. Studies investigate the impact of these technologies on environmental protection and increasing the resilience of infrastructure to climate change. Also, the implementation of advanced cybersecurity solutions is addressed in various studies. The implementation of these solutions in ports is essential for protecting critical infrastructure and logistics flows in a context where the digitization of maritime operations is constantly increasing. Modern ports rely on interconnected IT systems for maritime traffic management, automated crane operation, container monitoring, and supply chain coordination, making them sensitive targets for cyberattacks. To prevent major disruptions, technologies such as advanced firewalls, intrusion detection systems (IDS), intrusion prevention systems (IPS), multi-factor authentication solutions, data encryption, and continuous network monitoring using artificial intelligence are required. Staff training and the implementation of strict access management policies are also critical components. By adopting these measures, ports can strengthen their operational capabilities and ensure business continuity in the face of increasingly complex cyber threats. Overall, the literature highlights that the transformation of ports into smart ports is contingent upon the definition and implementation of clear strategic directions that integrate digital technologies, sustainability, and operational resilience into a coherent development framework. In this context, for Romanian seaports, the need to adopt well-founded strategies becomes essential for capitalizing on geostrategic advantages, increasing operational efficiency, and aligning with European and international trends. The coordinated implementation of IoT technologies, smart logistics solutions, OPSs, climate change-adapted technologies, and advanced cybersecurity measures can support the transition of Romanian ports to smart, competitive, and sustainable logistics hubs capable of responding to the economic, technological, and environmental challenges of the contemporary maritime industry.

normalized_text: , technological progress, and sustainability requirements. 2.3. The Digital Transformation Application of Eastern European Ports: The digital transformation of seaports in Eastern Europe is a strategic process undergoing rapid development, driven by global competitive pressures, sustainability requirements, and the need for integration into European and international logistics networks. Ports such as Constanta, Odessa, Gdansk, Gdynia, and Koper have initiated digitization processes aimed at modernizing IT infrastructure, implementing port management systems, using IoT sensors to monitor operations, and introducing digital platforms for data exchange. However, the pace of digital transformation in Eastern European seaports is uneven, influenced by investment levels, institutional maturity, and the availability of digital skills. Existing infrastructure, which is often heterogeneous and partially outdated, as well as challenges related to cybersecurity and organizational resistance to change, may limit the full exploitation of digitization benefits. Several studies discuss modernization and digitalization processes in Eastern European ports, highlighting both their potential and challenges. These works emphasize the role of emerging technologies such as IoT, AI, and data analytics in improving efficiency and sustainability, while also revealing differences in digital maturity across ports. Thus, studies evaluated sustainability strategies, digital transformation processes, smart port concepts, and communication systems in various ports, highlighting opportunities for modernization through advanced technologies such as AI, blockchain, and IoT. A central element of this transformation is the adoption of emerging technologies, such as AI, Big Data analytics, and process automation, which enable the optimization of cargo flows, reduction in operating times, and increased operational transparency. By collecting and analyzing data in real time, ports in Eastern Europe can improve operational planning, vessel traffic, and port resource management, and the ability to anticipate operational and environmental risks. At the same time, these solutions contribute to increasing cross- border digital interoperability, which is essential for the efficient functioning of intermodal transport corridors. By examining successful examples from various countries and the potential benefits of adopting “smart ports” for the maritime sector, adapted to the economy and environment in our country, this study aims to ensure a detailed evaluation of the best strategies that can facilitate the transition of Romanian seaports to the “smart port” model, and offers relevant recommendations for port authorities and other stakeholders in the domain. Implementing sustainable, advanced, and efficient port operations can support a nation’s economy and environment in the long term by boosting economic development, reducing logistics costs, increasing international competitiveness, and strengthening environmental protection and climate change adaptation efforts.

Progress is uneven and depends on investment levels, institutional maturity, and digital skills; heterogeneous, partially outdated infrastructure, cybersecurity challenges, and organizational resistance may limit benefits. heading_path: Main > 3 Research Methodology > General
normalized_text: The complexity of developing Romanian seaports as smart ports requires a systematic approach, involving a thorough analysis of internal and external environmental factors, as well as the development of strategies to be implemented to achieve this objective. The use of the SWOT method, combined with the formulation of strategies based on the identification of environmental factors, proves essential for determining the most suitable strategies in the process of ports transforming into “smart ports”. In addition, in most cases, the strategic planning process requires consideration of the interdependencies between the effects of external and internal factors. In order to identify the most appropriate port strategies in the context of the transition of the analyzed ports to smart ports, in addition to the information obtained by the authors from the annual reports of the analyzed ports, consultations were also held with experts in the domain of maritime ports. We believe that this has resulted in a rigorous assessment of the set of weaknesses, strengths, threats, and opportunities of these ports, providing a solid foundation for the development of innovative strategies. The strategies formulated on the basis of the questionnaire responses and interviews with port experts were then validated by these specialists, a crucial aspect in ensuring the viability and efficiency of their implementation in the specific context of the ports analyzed, as they were considered significant and useful for transforming ports into smart ports. This collaboration between theory and practice not only facilitates the identification of effective solutions but also aligns them with international best practices, thus contributing to the modernization and optimization of port operations. Thus, the results of the strategic process will have a direct impact on the ability of ports to become smart logistics centers, efficiently integrated into global transport networks. Therefore, to carry out an analysis of the seaports in our country, to define the most appropriate strategies for their development as “smart ports”, the SWOT-AHP hybrid method was used, which, being a subjective method, does not require a very large number of respondents. Based on the literature studied on the application of this hybrid method in the case of ports aiming to prioritize the defined strategies to establish which of them are priorities in the case of Romanian seaports, a questionnaire appropriate to the proposed approach was designed. The questionnaire was distributed electronically in October 2025 to a number of sixty-three study participants, with experience in the port domain ranging from 5 to 20 years, who are members of MPAC, directors of these ports, and members of various departments belonging to the three ports studied. Of the study participants who were contacted, only fifty-nine responses

heading_path: Main > 5. Discussions on the Level of Implementation of the Strategies Identified in Romanian Seaports > General
normalized_text: Transforming Romanian seaports into smart ports requires a systemic approach based on emerging technologies, digital governance, and continuous performance monitoring. The strategies defined, which are to be implemented in Romanian seaports, must be aligned with European objectives for decarbonization, digitalization, and geopolitical resilience, and supported by clear long-term monitoring mechanisms. In order for these strategies defined in the study to be implemented, a long-term monitoring system is required, which involves establishing planning stages, allocating resources, defining performance indicators, and mechanisms for continuous progress evaluation and feedback. These strategies must also include measures to adapt to future technological changes, sustainability policies, and the active involvement of relevant actors in the port ecosystem (authorities, economic operators, and local communities) for effective and sustainable implementation. Such an approach would help ensure the sustainability of investments and the continuous optimization of port processes in relation to smart development strategic objectives. continuous progress evaluation and feedback. These strategies must also include measures to adapt to future technological changes, sustainability policies, and the active involvement of relevant actors in the port ecosystem (authorities, economic operators, and local communities) for effective and sustainable implementation. Such an approach would help ensure the sustainability of investments and the continuous optimization of port processes in relation to smart development strategic objectives. Implementing the defined strategies and monitoring the smart port transformation is a complex process, but achievable in our country through the following: • A clear vision (establishing what “smart” means for each port: increased operational efficiency, digitization of logistics flows, port safety and security, energy and environmental sustainability, integration with national and European infrastructure). • Coordinated governance, which involves setting up an inter-institutional committee that includes the following: port authorities, representatives of relevant ministries (Transport, Environment, Economy), terminal operators, IT providers, and the local logistics community. • Solid technological architecture, by conducting a detailed audit of existing capacities (IT infrastructure, traffic management systems, IoT sensors and equipment, level of interoperability between systems) and defining a coherent technical framework (central data platform, robust cybersecurity, interoperability with the systems of the authorities and the port community). • Projects implemented in several phases: phase 1—pilot projects with rapid impact and moderate cost (traffic and logistics monitoring sensors, container tracking systems (track-and-trace), digitization of customs documents), accompanied by tests to validate the technology and processes; phase 2—scaling and integration (expanding functionalities at port level, integration with regional logistics and rail/road systems, connection with partner ports in the European network); phase 3—continuous innovation (automation solutions, integration with smart city systems, adoption of AI for predictive optimization). • Long-term monitoring as a strategic governance mechanism, with the definition of key performance indicators (KPIs) that enable continuous assessment of the impact of digitization and automation, informed investment decisions, increased transparency towards authorities and financiers, and rapid adaptation to technological, economic, or legislative changes. • Continuous adaptation and innovation through the adoption of innovative management models. For Romanian seaports, monitoring must take into account their strategic role in the TEN-T network, high intermodality, environmental pressures, and EU requirements on decarbonization.

In Romania, the main seaport (Constanta) is in the planning phase for OPS infrastructure, which has been included in port digitization and decarbonization strategies as part of the process of integration into the European green port network within the TEN-T, although there are challenges related to the lack of sufficient electrical infrastructure, the need to standardize equipment on board ships, and a long investment payback period. Implementation of Smart Technologies (SO2): The second strategy, implementing smart technologies in ports, is essential for: • Increasing competitiveness (ports attract more operators and become preferred logistics hubs in global networks); • Increasing port operations efficiency (reducing ship dwell times and optimizing processes); • Reducing costs (through predictive maintenance and optimization of resource use); • Reducing environmental impact (through monitoring and reducing emissions, implementing green solutions); • Security and transparency (through real-time data collection and sharing that improves control and reduces logistical risks). Therefore, this strategy represents a decisive step towards efficiency, sustainability, and logistical resilience, as it is not only a technical modernization but also an organizational and logistical paradigm change. The implementation of this strategy also poses challenges, such as high initial investments, especially for digital infrastructure, resistance to change, and the need for professional training for port staff, but also the need to ensure cybersecurity and system interoperability, as ports operate with different IT platforms. Among the European ports that have implemented smart technologies are the Antwerp-Bruges Port in Belgium (digital twin, autonomous drones, and a private 5G network for operations control), the Rotterdam Port in the Netherlands (which has integrated IoT and a platform for data sharing between logistics actors) and the port of Tallinn in Estonia (which has the “Smart Port Solution” system for optimizing vehicle access and traffic management). These examples demonstrate that port digitalization is not just a theoretical option, but a practical reality in modern European ports. For Romanian seaports, these examples of “smart ports” can be a source of inspiration for what can be done in terms of implementing smart technologies that are adapted to the size and infrastructure of the port, as well as to the financing possibilities. The success of this strategy depends on phased planning, gradual testing of technological solutions, close cooperation between the port authority and private partners, and the consistent integration of sustainability principles.

Promotion of Logistics Services to Improve Port Infrastructure (SO3): The strategy for promoting logistics services to improve port infrastructure represents a modern, integrated, and sustainable approach to port development. By adding economic value, optimizing infrastructure, and stimulating cooperation between actors, ports can become competitive regional logistics centers capable of responding to global market demands. In this way, storage, packaging, labeling, or even pre-assembly activities add value to cargo and reduce transport costs and times. The quality of port infrastructure and the existence of efficient logistics services directly influence the decisions of traders and transporters to use a port, which means that logistics becomes a determining factor in port competitiveness. Thus, in an economic environment based on connectivity, efficiency, and sustainability, the development of logistics services becomes the key to modernizing port infrastructure. Among the challenges of implementing this strategy are: lack of capital for investment, deficiencies in access infrastructure—road, rail, and river—which limit the connectivity of ports with hinterland areas, reducing the region’s logistical competitiveness, the risk that logistics services will be implemented without real correlation with port infrastructure, which reduces efficiency and operational synergy, diminishing the integrated performance of the logistics chain, lack of coordination between the actors involved (port authorities, logistics operators, transporters, and local administrations), challenges related to digitization (many ports have not yet implemented integrated IT systems or logistics management platforms), the need for qualified logistics personnel, competition from other ports, slow administrative regulations and customs procedures, and increasingly stringent sustainability requirements. This direction is particularly important for Romania’s seaports, as it allows them to capitalize on their strategic geographical position and integrate into international logistics networks. Due to infrastructure limitations and the lack of efficient connections between ports, railways, and existing road networks, lead to high logistics costs and losses in competitiveness. Therefore, the successful implementation of this strategy in our country depends on a coordinated approach to all factors, the integration of services with infrastructure, inter-institutional collaboration, digitization, and sustainability, which together can ensure coherent and sustainable port development. Port logistics services can thus become an important vector of growth and efficiency for Romanian seaports.

heading_path: Main > 5. Discussions on the Level of Implementation of the Strategies Identified in Romanian Seaports > General
normalized_text: 5.5. Implementation of IoT Technologies (SO5): In the context of increasing freight volumes and the globalization of logistics chains, ports are becoming critical infrastructure hubs for economic competitiveness and sustainability. The implementation of IoT technologies in ports (SO5) is an essential strategy for transforming them into “smart ports”, with facilities capable of collecting real-time data, optimizing traffic flows, reducing operational costs, and minimizing environmental impact. A study conducted on the port of Hamburg shows that the integration of IoT technologies, in the context of space constraints and increasing traffic, is vital for increasing productivity and reducing negative external impacts (traffic, pollution). Implementing IoT technology in a port involves installing sensors (traffic, infrastructure, and environment), communication networks (NB-IoT, 5G, LoRaWAN), and data processing platforms, as well as connecting equipment (cranes, vehicles, wagons), integrating infrastructure monitoring systems, and collecting and analyzing data for operational and strategic decisions. By using IoT technologies and data analysis, ports can anticipate congestion, streamline ship berthing schedules, and monitor infrastructure status. Furthermore, the implementation of IoT technologies requires a robust approach to security from the design phase (“security by design”), well-defined governance, network separation, and effective incident detection and response mechanisms. Ports that adopt IoT can gain significant competitive advantages: reduced waiting times, optimized internal logistics and energy consumption, real-time monitoring of activities and infrastructure, and reduced environmental impact. However, cooperation between the port authority, operators, technology providers, and the logistics community within a common governance framework is essential for success. Cybersecurity must also be integrated into the IoT strategy from the outset, as vulnerabilities can compromise port operations. With regard to the Port of Constanta, as a key hub for maritime transport infrastructure in Romania and Southeast Europe, digital transformation is crucial for increasing sustainability, efficiency, and transparency. The Port of Constanta faces several major challenges, being identified as the only one of the top ten European ports that is not digitized to the same level as its competitors, which highlights the urgency of implementing the IoT strategy. For the successful use of this technology, it is first necessary to develop a clear roadmap setting out the stages of IoT technology implementation, defining KPIs key performance indicators, running pilot projects, and ensuring scalability. Secondly, it is necessary to involve all port stakeholders (port authority, operators, carriers, logistics experts) in a common data platform and to allocate European funds to cover the initial costs. Thirdly,

Thirdly, it is vital to ensure system-level interoperability, cybersecurity, and data protection, as well as constant monitoring of environmental impact and communication of results to attract capital and credibility. 5.6. Development of Training Programs for Digital Skills (WO1): “Smart ports” rely on the integration of digital technologies (IoT, AI, blockchain, big data, automation) to increase operational security, efficiency, and sustainability. The strategy of developing training programs for digital skills (WO1) has several advantages: port workers, managers, and IT staff can use digital solutions (traffic management platforms, sensors, predictive systems, etc.) more efficiently; it increases the acceptance of these new technologies and encourages a culture of innovation if clear and continuous training is provided to port employees, while reducing the risks of human error and incidents; increases the attractiveness of Romanian ports because digitally trained staff can provide faster and more transparent services, attracting investors and international shipping lines; alignment with EU policies. The risks of this strategy for Romanian seaports include the following: a shortage of specialized trainers (Romania still has a gap between the needs of the maritime industry and the educational offer); significant initial costs (development of modern programs, digital laboratories, partnerships with maritime and IT universities); the need for constant updating (digital skills become obsolete quickly, so training must be continuous). The effective implementation of this strategy in Romanian seaports requires an assessment of existing digital skills, the development of tailored modules (ranging from software use, data analysis, to AI/automation), establishing key roles that require digital skills, implementing pilot programs, integration into the overall port digitization strategy, robust partnerships (with universities or technology training centers), funding, and continuous evaluation. In a context marked by globalization, intensified logistics competition, and the implementation of the European digital agenda, Romanian seaports that prioritize investment in digital human capital will make significant progress in terms of operational efficiency, competitiveness, and long-term sustainability.

In a context marked by globalization, intensified logistics competition, and the implementation of the European digital agenda, Romanian seaports that prioritize investment in digital human capital will make significant progress in terms of operational efficiency, competitiveness, and long-term sustainability. 5.7. Improving Cybersecurity (WO2): Global maritime transport is becoming increasingly digitized, and port infrastructures are becoming increasingly connected, making them vulnerable to cyber attacks. At the European level, maritime security explicitly integrates the cyber component, recognizing critical infrastructure in maritime operations, including ports and submarine data cables, as areas of high vulnerability and strategic importance. In Romania, seaports are critical hubs from a logistics and trade perspective, but also from a strategic/regional perspective. Therefore, the strategy to improve cybersecurity in Romanian seaports (WO2) is not only justified but urgent, as it is essential for Romania’s economic and strategic security. The strategy must include risk assessment, technical and procedural measures, training, cooperation, and adequate investment. Romania already has several initiatives underway, but a consolidated and sustainable effort is needed to ensure resilience against future cyber-attacks. The Port of Constanta uses complex IT systems for: maritime traffic management (Vessel Traffic Services), logistics and loading/unloading operations, access and electricity control, communications, and coordination with customs and port authorities. According to exercises carried out in the port of Constanta, the most relevant vulnerabilities include the following: the lack of a unified IT/OT (“Operational Technology”) cyber infrastructure, outdated SCADA processing (“Supervisory Control & Data Acquisition”) and industrial control systems, limited access to personnel specialized in maritime cybersecurity, and the absence of a dedicated security operations center (SOC). To address these vulnerabilities, the port of Constanta plans to: increase the cyber security of IT/OT infrastructures in ports (traffic control systems, control towers, ship access, port logistics), reduce cyber-attacks, through protection, authentication, segmentation, continuous monitoring, ensuring inter-organizational cooperation between port operators, state authorities, IT providers, the maritime community, according to the international/EU standards and regulations (“IMO-International Maritime Organization”, “EU Critical Infrastructure Rules”), develop response and recovery capabilities in the event of a cyber incident (exercises, simulations, continuity plans), promote a culture of security (awareness, education, training) among port and logistics personnel.

Implementation of the Climate Change Adaptation Technologies (ST1): Seaports are exposed to an increased set of risks generated by climate change: rising sea levels, more frequent and intense storms, heat waves, coastal erosion, and flooding, but also indirect impacts on logistics chains. In addition, ports are critical infrastructure: any malfunction of a port platform, pier, dock, or logistics system can have major economic repercussions. Based on these factors, it is strategic for ports to adopt technologies adapted to climate change, not only to reduce emissions but also for adaptation and resilience. In this regard, the global port sector has been invited, through international initiatives, to adopt climate adaptation measures (“resilience”) and dedicated technologies. Therefore, the strategy for implementing climate-adapted technologies (ST1) in the port sector is not only “good practice” but essential for the safety, continuity, and competitiveness of ports globally and locally. Among the technologies adapted to climate change in ports, the following can be identified: climate monitoring and prediction/digital twin/IoT sensors (systems for monitoring sea level, erosion, the state of port infrastructure, temperature changes, storms, etc., to anticipate the necessary interventions); digital technologies for resilient operations (just-in-time operating technologies, reducing ship waiting times and optimizing flows, contributing to efficiency and reducing exposure to climate risks); adapted physical infrastructure/resilient materials (quays and docks that can withstand storms and sea level rise, selection of materials resistant to marine corrosion and extreme conditions, flood-adapted protective barriers); integration of “nature-based” solutions and holistic planning (use of coastal areas to absorb shocks (reefs, “living” shores), solutions that can complement traditional infrastructure). In Romania, seaports are located on the Black Sea, an area at risk of sea level rise, climate variations, and intense storm phenomena, which require adaptation to climate risk scenarios. Implementing climate change adaptation technologies would mean for Romania: auditing the several vulnerabilities of the port infrastructure to sea level rise, rehabilitating quays/docks to withstand flooding, implementing IoT sensors for real-time monitoring of structural condition, and integrating climate scenarios into port management systems.

Strengthening International Partnerships for the Resilience of Romanian Seaports in the Context of Geopolitical Tensions (ST2): The Black Sea region is facing intensified geopolitical dynamics, generated by the war in Ukraine, the reconfiguration of global logistics chains, and competition between major powers for influence in infrastructure and transport. In this context, Romanian seaports are becoming strategic points not only economically, but also geopolitically. The strategy of strengthening international partnerships (with states, organizations, investors) (ST2) thus takes on major importance in ensuring both commercial continuity and the security of critical infrastructure. For Romania to become a hub of stability and development in the Black Sea region, this strategy must combine active economic diplomacy, investment in infrastructure and digitalization, security, and sustainability. critical infrastructure.
