heading_path: Main > 5 Conclusions > General
normalized_text: Conclusions: The paper proposes an analysis, from the point of view of stability, of microgrid operation in the case of grid-connection via SST, which can be seen as an essential enabler of microgrids-by-design. This solution allows by design, the accommodation of large quantities of distributed renewables production, the friendly connection to the main grid (predictability and no disturbance propagation in both directions), while enhancing the resilience or even reaching immunity for the end-users supplied by the respective microgrid. The advantages have been analyzed with two test scenarios, each with two types of disturbances in the microgrid—one considering the sudden connection of a load, and another one introduced by the sudden loss of a constant power supplied from the main grid. The simulations were performed in Matlab/Simulink version R2017b. A simplified microgrid was assumed, with only three loads (two modelled as constant real power P and modelled as constant impedance Z) and one PV plant assumed to produce constant power (PPV = ct.), all connected to a low voltage line, as depicted. The microgrid is connected to the bulk power system through an SST, consisting of the grid-connected inverter and the DC bus where the capacitor is connected, and the battery based storage system. The control of the SST output was also considered, namely through a cascaded controller in which the AC voltages (filter elements) and AC currents (inductor of filter) are controlled. A controller for the PV system was also considered. In this case was again adopted a cascade structure, but what is controlled is the DC voltage of the inverter and their AC currents. The bulk power system is modeled as a power source that supplies a constant P to the DC bus-bar. Future work will consider more complex situations—requiring eventually for further elaboration of algorithms; however, the proof of concept has been demonstrated with the simplified grid. Moreover, future work will consider microgrid stabilization in wider time frames (minutes, hours or more), as being related to the battery and PV (microgrid local production) resources size in the microgrid. The results of the numerical simulations consider a Decentralized Energy Supply System based on the SST connected microgrid which becomes immune and resilient towards power supply failures and for blackouts. The simulation shows the level of resilience of the microgrid in this situation and the capacity to remain stable without mechanical inertia, but based on the electrostatic energy in capacitors behind the inverters, acting as

The results consider an SST-connected microgrid that becomes immune and resilient toward power supply failures and blackouts.
compacted_text: The paper analyzes microgrid stability for grid connection via an SST, described as an enabler of microgrids-by-design. It states this approach can accommodate large amounts of distributed renewable generation, provide friendly connection to the main grid with predictability and no disturbance propagation in both directions, and enhance resilience or even immunity for end-users. Two test scenarios were studied: sudden connection of a load and sudden loss of constant power supplied from the main grid. Simulations were performed in Matlab/Simulink R2017b on a simplified microgrid with three loads, one PV plant producing constant power, and connection to the bulk power system through an SST with a grid-connected inverter, DC bus capacitor, and battery-based storage. Cascaded controllers were used for the SST output and PV system. The results indicate the SST-connected microgrid can remain

heading_path: Main > 5 Conclusions > General
normalized_text: results of the numerical simulations consider a Decentralized Energy Supply System based on the SST connected microgrid which becomes immune and resilient towards power supply failures and for blackouts. The simulation shows the level of resilience of the microgrid in this situation and the capacity to remain stable without mechanical inertia, but based on the electrostatic energy in capacitors behind the inverters, acting as system inertia to absorb the unbalance between loads and production. One important lesson is that the role of the mechanical inertia in the main grids is taken in the full inverter-based microgrid by the energy in the capacitors on the DC busbar behind the inverters, especially from the SST as grid former, and that the dimension of this capacitor needs to be chosen in order to cope with the disturbance level. In the studied microgrids, there is no frequency dependency for the grid stability, but only voltage control in the DC bus-bars of the injectors and PVs injecting power in the microgrid used in our test cases), which allows to keep stability during the transients based on the released energy from the capacitors on the DC bus-bars behind the inverters. The two scenarios show that bigger capacitors give better stability of DC busbar voltage and of the microgrid, while smaller capacitors are still able to maintain stability against disturbances, even if the DC busbar voltage has a higher variation. We extend the simulation case studies: To address some variations regarding the PV generation, and also to examine how some disturbances for the grid side are observed in the microgrid side. In that way, we can claim that the main transient events that can occur in the daily operation of the microgrid have been adequately examined. These critical transient events are not a problem considering a wider time frame because in this case the stability of the system is only dependent on the battery and PV size. Future work will expand the idea on the applicability and efficacy of shared resources for ad-hoc formed clusters of sub-microgrids connected physically or by cyber connection links. Moreover, more complex use-cases in terms of microgrid complexity and with more types of disturbances will be further analyzed, also by using new methods of enhancing its real-time balancing and stability.

The SST connected microgrid becomes immune and resilient toward power supply failures and blackouts.
The simulation evaluates the microgrid's resilience and its ability to remain stable without mechanical inertia.
The microgrid stability is supported by electrostatic energy in capacitors behind the inverters acting as system inertia.
In a full inverter-based microgrid, the energy in DC busbar capacitors behind the inverters takes the role of mechanical inertia in main grids.
The capacitor dimension must be chosen to cope with the disturbance level.
In the studied microgrids, grid stability depends on voltage control in DC bus-bars rather than frequency dependency.
Bigger capacitors provide better stability of DC busbar voltage and microgrid behavior, while smaller capacitors still maintain stability despite higher DC busbar voltage variation.
Future work will expand shared resources for ad-hoc formed clusters of sub-microgrids and analyze more complex microgrid use-cases with additional disturbance types.
compacted_text: Numerical simulations of an SST-connected decentralized energy supply microgrid show resilience to power supply failures and blackouts, and stable operation without mechanical inertia. System inertia is provided by electrostatic energy in capacitors on the DC busbar behind the inverters, especially the SST as grid former, which absorbs load-production imbalance; capacitor size must be chosen to cope with disturbance level. In the studied microgrids, stability depends on DC busbar voltage control, not frequency dependency. Larger capacitors improve DC busbar voltage stability and microgrid stability; smaller capacitors still maintain stability under disturbances but with higher DC busbar voltage variation. The case studies also examined PV generation variations and grid-side disturbances observed on the microgrid side; over a wider time frame, stability depends only on battery and PV

Note 1 to entry: Different types of devices could be defined depending on the nature of the installation, DC/DC or AC/DC, unidirectional or bidirectional. Note 2 to entry: This is also called Active Front-End Converter (AFE), or Active Infeed Converter (AIC) in DC-Industrie installations. [SOURCE: JWG44] NOTE: New standard for Interlink Converters (ILC) under development by SC22E-WG12 (future IEC 63532. 4.1.13 Intertripping the tripping of circuit-breaker(s) by signals initiated from protection at a remote location independent of the state of the local protection. [SOURCE: IEC 60050-448, 448-15-08] under consideration by TC64/JWG44 for IEC 60364-8-82. Intertripping enables safe and simultaneous disconnection of all power sources in a DC installation or in a cell of the installation. 4.1.14 Leakage Current Electric current in an unintended conductive path under normal conditions. NOTE 1 to entry: Normal condition means the condition in which all means of protection are intact (IEV 903-02-07). NOTE 2 to entry: The same term is also used for the electric current flowing across a semiconductor device in non-conducting mode at a specified voltage. NOTE 3 to entry: the unintended conductive path can include unpredictable electrolytic conduction, e.g. in soil and concrete. normalized_text: Operations 5.5.1 System Startup DC prosumer installations usually include equipment containing capacitors that draw a large in-rush current when powered-on. Without further measures these in-rush currents would trip the protection devices and cause voltage dips or interruptions in weak source applications. Thus, a predefined startup sequence is needed. In Current/OS, system startup consists of three phases: • black start, to recharge the capacitance in the DC-bus, • to precharge the stray capacitance of the connection cable between the DC-bus and the equipment, • to precharge the internal capacitance of the equipment. Startup is typically a hierarchical top-down procedure. A bottom-up procedure can be considered for islanded grids with energy storage in a subcircuit. 5.5.2 System Primary Control Primary control, or droop control, is a local control loop on the power converters, regulating the current flow according to the voltage level sensed and a droop curve, with the goal of balancing the power input and output in the grid. Primary control has a characteristic time in the order of milliseconds and operates continuously adjusting the setpoint value of the primary control. A voltage exceeding the middle-band or “nominal” voltage Un indicates that the system is in “oversupply”. When in this condition, loads are activated according to priority settings, power is stored in batteries, or in thermal storage systems (hot water, fridges and cold rooms, HVAC storage, etc.), or by generating hydrogen. When voltage is below Un, the system is in “undersupply”. In this condition: • loads are deactivated according to priority settings, • electrical storage feeds power to the grid, • thermal storage is inactive. The voltage-dependent behavior of the equipment is defined by the droop curves below, with the Power in the horizontal axis, and the Voltage in the vertical axis. Linear Drop Control Stepwise control mode is not suitable for bigger loads, or aggregations of multiple small loads simultaneously managed (e.g. lighting), and a smoother reaction is necessary, as: • the load power has significant impact on the grid stability. • the load can contribute to the power consumption adjustment. In such cases, the drop curve consists of a linear transition from full power at an upper voltage threshold, to zero at a lower threshold. The two voltage thresholds are settings that can be adapted to each prosumer (see installation) case.

In such cases, the drop curve consists of a linear transition from full power at an upper voltage threshold, to zero at a lower threshold. The two voltage thresholds are settings that can be adapted to each prosumer (see installation) case. The two voltage values at both ends of the slope are settings that can be adapted to each prosumer installation case. 5.5.2.4 Source Management with Droops When it comes to simple sources such as Photovoltaic (PV), systems can behave with a similar response to voltage: the source will provide full power until a certain defined maximum voltage. Above such voltage, power generated will reduce in a linear way to zero. The two voltage values at both ends of the slope are settings that can be adapted to each prosumer installation case.

normalized_text: DC Zoning System In order to ease design and operation of DC installations, circuits or group of circuits are classified according to the available fault energy and the protection means in 6 DC Zones, labeled as 0, 1, 2.1, 2.2, 3 and 4, described in the following sections. IEC 60364 standards apply to all DC Zones. 5.6.1 DC Zone 0 Main characteristics of the circuits: High short-circuit current source with no overcurrent protection. Main consequences: • High fault current, • High incident energy in case of arc flash. • Circuits cannot be distributed but must be segregated in panels or in rooms with restricted access.. 5.6.2 DC Zone 1 Main characteristics of the circuits • Most sources have high prospective short-circuit current. • Overcurrent protection devices (OCPD) on the DC bus have breaking time < 50ms (e.g.: EMCBs and fuses). • Multiple sources, all connected to a common distribution board. Main consequences • High fault current • High incident energy in case of arc flash. • Circuits should not be distributed but segregated in panels or in spaces with restricted access. • Design cannot be replicated and easily upgraded. 5.6.3 DC Zone 2.1 Main characteristics of the circuits • Sources cannot deliver a current significantly higher than its nominal current, by design. • OCPD with breaking time < 50ms (e.g.: EMCBs and fuses) on the DC bus. • Energy stored in capacitors does not exceed 600 J. • Multiple sources connected to a single distribution board. Main consequences • Design of protection only by skilled engineers. • Designs difficult to be replicated and upgraded. • Arc-flash incident energy lower than in DC Zone 1 but requiring assessment. • Limited system availability as selectivity is generally not possible. • Limited design flexibility as sources cannot be distributed. • Suitable for installations with limited complexity. 5.6.4 DC Zone 2.2 Main characteristics of the circuits

normalized_text: Primary Control Primary control, or droop control, is a local control loop on the power converters, regulating the current flow according to the voltage level sensed and a droop curve, with the goal of balancing the power input and output in the grid. Primary control has a characteristic time in the order of milliseconds and operates continuously adjusting the setpoint value of the primary control. A voltage exceeding the middle-band or “nominal” voltage Un indicates that the system is in “oversupply”. When in this condition, loads shall activate according to priority settings, power shall be stored in batteries, or in thermal storage systems (hot water, fridges and cold rooms, HVAC storage, etc.), or by generating hydrogen. When voltage is below Un, the system is in “undersupply”. In this condition: • loads shall de-activate according to priority settings, • electrical storage shall feed power to the grid, • thermal storage shall be inactive. The voltage-dependent behavior of the equipment is defined by the droop curves below, with the Power in the horizontal axis, and the Voltage in the vertical axis. condition: • loads shall de-activate according to priority settings, • electrical storage shall feed power to the grid, • thermal storage shall be inactive. The voltage-dependent behavior of the equipment is defined by the droop curves below, with the Power in the horizontal axis, and the Voltage in the vertical axis. Droop control can be stepwise (8.3.3.1) or continuous (8.3.3.2). Sources shall have linear droop, with the exception of sources, typically the interlink converter and/or stationary batteries, intended to introduce synthetic inertia, that may have non-linear droop. Small loads, whose nominal power do not exceed the power that can be delivered by the smallest source on the grid in the minimal system configuration, can have stepwise droop. All other loads shall have continuous droop. Groups of small loads operated in a coordinated way with the same voltage threshold, e.g. a group of lamps switched simultaneously, shall be considered as a dominant load. NOTE: small loads are also called non-significant or non-dominant loads, as their activation and deactivation do not affect the stability of the system control. NOTE: in a grid-connected system with battery, the smallest source is typically the battery and the reference value for this classification is the nominal power of the battery converter. The power delivered by the interlink converter and the PV shall not be considered. NOTE: For instance, a water heater in an individual house has significant impact on the system stability (typically a 2 kW water heater connected to a 9 kW prosumer installation). 8.3.3.1 Stepwise Control Small loads managed with stepwise control are turned ON when the Voltage exceeds a predefined threshold value Uthreshold, and turned OFF when the voltage drops below this value. Priority between loads can be defined by assigning different voltage thresholds to different classes of loads: • lower voltage thresholds to higher priority loads, e.g. emergency lights; • higher voltage thresholds are assigned to non-priority loads, to save power for other loads in high-demand or reduced power availability periods. An example with different priority levels is. Implementation rules detailed in Current/OS System Reference Document. Where a number of small loads are expected to be switched on/off simultaneously, the on/off-slope time must be multiplied by the number of expected paralleled devices. Slow ramp-up/down allows distinguishing normal switch-on/off of devices from faults with high dl/dt and improves system stability.

normalized_text: Linear Droop Control Stepwise control mode is not suitable for bigger loads, or aggregations of multiple small loads simultaneously managed (e.g. lighting), and a smoother reaction is necessary, as: • the load power has significant impact on the grid stability. • the load can contribute to the power consumption adjustment. In such cases, the droop curve consists of a linear transition from full power at an upper voltage threshold, to zero at a lower threshold. The two voltage thresholds are settings that can be adapted to each prosumer (see installation case). NOTE: In the case of the water heater, the power could be 2 kW above 700 VDC, 0 kW below 640 VDC and reduced in between the two values. In such cases, water heating will just take longer. Droop curves for sources can include a dead-band. More details in Current/OS System Reference Document. The droop controller gain must be determined in a way that it guarantees system stability. The maximum allowable voltage variations as well as the stability of the closed loop system are the factors that must be taken into account in the droop gain design procedure. Higher droop gains can reduce the effect of line resistances on the current sharing accuracy. Lower droop gains cause mismatches among the output currents. However, higher droop gains cause large voltage drops in the output terminal of the converters. NOTE: droop gain can be in Ohms like Ri = V2/P or Ri = V/I, but it could also be VxP During the installation phase, droop settings could be adapted to ensure or improve stability and to adjust the priorities of the different circuits according to the application specifications. Therefore, the possibility to adjust droops must be implemented in any variant of the sources/significant loads. When adding a new source to the system, its droop needs to be adapted according to the system architecture to avoid instabilities. The following parameters of droop curves need to be adjustable: • Nominal voltage Unom; • Deadband width Udb; • Voltages Ux2 and Ux3

Above such voltage, power generated will reduce in a linear way to zero. The two voltage values at both ends of the slope are settings that can be adapted to each prosumer installation case. Managed Bidirectional Devices with Droops Managed bidirectional devices can be battery storage, or bi-directional electrical vehicle (EV) chargers with vehicle to grid (V2G) capability, or bi-directional AC/DC grid interlink converters. NOTE: Motor systems with variable-speed drives, having reverse power capability due to power regeneration in braking, are not considered as the reverse power generation is not controllable and dependable. Bidirectional circuits such as electrical storage will have a configured voltage band where the system will be inactive. Above this “dead band”, the power used to charge the batteries or feedback the AC system will increase linearly up to the nominal charge power. Below this “dead band”, the power delivered to the DC grid from the batteries’ discharge, or the AC system will increase linearly up to the nominal supply power. This case is detailed. NOTE 1: For battery-storage systems, the power flow is managed by the converter according to the SoC and other parameters communicated by the BMS monitoring the batteries. The droop curves are intended to provide limiting values. Similar considerations apply to V2G chargers. All slopes and thresholds can be configured through a communication system. 8.3.3.4.4 Load Priorities Management with Droops As already stated, all droop curves can be defined with dedicated settings as it will be shown in the examples below. normalized_text: Tertiary Control – Power Flow and Energy Management High level control intended for the overall energy management of the system. Based purely on a centralized control of the system. Control bandwidth is very low. Possibility for DSO (distribution system operator) to communicate and adjust the system. Transmission system operator (TSO) can also request DSO for ancillary services. Droop settings are intended to be dynamically managed for optimizing energy use. Batteries can be forced to discharge at a certain period for peak shaving or high electricity price reasons. By changing droop settings, the same battery can be forced to charge at another period to store PV exceeding power generation, or during the night when the electricity price is low. As energy optimization requires data at system level and, possibly, external information (e.g. electricity prices, weather forecasts, etc.), it is managed by a centralized control system, controlling droop setting of loads and sources through a communication protocol.
statements: Tertiary control is intended for overall energy management of the system using centralized control.
Droop settings are dynamically managed to optimize energy use.
Batteries can be forced to discharge during a certain period for peak shaving or high electricity price reasons.
A centralized control system manages droop settings of loads and sources using system-level data and possibly external information.
compacted_text: Tertiary control is a high-level, centralized control for overall energy management with very low control bandwidth. The DSO can communicate with and adjust the system, and the TSO can request DSO ancillary services. Droop settings are dynamically managed to optimize energy use; batteries can be forced to discharge for peak shaving or high electricity price reasons, or to charge to store excess PV power or during low-price night periods. Energy optimization uses system-level data and possibly external information such as electricity prices and weather forecasts, and controls droop settings of loads and sources through a communication protocol.

DC Zone 2.2 - Multiple distributed sources. - Sources (or chuck) that limit the fault current rise and peak. - OCPD with breaking time < 1ms (e.g.: SCHCBs) on the DC bus. - Capacitor discharge is limited - Sources can be distributed. - Reduced arc-flash incident energy. - Selectivity is possible but difficult to design. - Designs are replicable, but difficult design upgrades require care. - Design by skilled design offices. - Suitable for complex installations (after design by skilled engineers). - Additional protection possible with RCD - Mid-point earthing makes maintenance simpler. Assessment is typically non required. - Design is limited to nominal current ranges. All Current/OS voltages as per Section 5.1 can be used in all DC Zones. Different installation rules can apply in different DC Zones.

heading_path: Main > 9 Protection > 9.1.1 DC Zone 0 - Unprotected Sources

acronyms.AFE: This is also called Active Front-End Converter
acronyms.PEI: Circuits in a DC Prosumer Electrical Installation
acronyms.BPBF: UPBF or UPF if PV source

normalized_text: DC Zone 0 - Unprotected Sources DC Zone 0 contains high-power voltage sources, such as multiple linked batteries or batteries with large energy content, or other sources with similar behavior during the fault, e.g. the public electricity grid, capacitor and supercapacitor banks, and the circuit connecting those sources to the protective device. Circuits downstream power converters with large capacitors are also classified in DC Zone Clause 433.3 of IEC 60364-4-43:2008 applies. Characteristics of DC Zone 0 are: • bidirectional power flow is possible, • very high fault current and energy, • high arc-flash incident energy. As arc-flash risk can be very high, DC Zones 0 circuits should have limited extension and be housed in an enclosure, e.g. in a battery rack or in a panel, or in a restricted room, and should be only accessible to highly skilled people, wearing appropriate Personal Protection Equipment (PPE). NFPA-70E or equivalent standards for arc flash should be considered.
statements: DC Zone 0 contains high-power voltage sources such as multiple linked batteries or batteries with large energy content, and other sources with similar behavior during the fault.
Clause 433.3 of IEC 60364-4-43:2008 applies to DC Zone 0.
DC Zone 0 circuits have bidirectional power flow possible, very high fault current and energy, and high arc-flash incident energy.

DC Zone 0 circuits should have limited extension and be housed in an enclosure or restricted room and be accessible only to highly skilled people wearing appropriate Personal Protection Equipment (PPE).
compacted_text: DC Zone 0 contains high-power voltage sources such as multiple linked batteries, batteries with large energy content, the public electricity grid, capacitor and supercapacitor banks, and the circuit connecting those sources to the protective device. Circuits downstream of power converters with large capacitors are also classified in DC Zone 0. IEC 60364-4-43:2008 Clause 433.3 applies. Characteristics: bidirectional power flow is possible, very high fault current and energy, and high arc-flash incident energy. Because arc-flash risk can be very high, DC Zone 0 circuits should have limited extension, be housed in an enclosure, restricted room, battery rack, or panel, and be accessible only to highly skilled people wearing appropriate PPE. NFPA-70E or equivalent arc-flash standards should be considered.

heading_path: Main > 9 Protection > 9.1.2 DC Zone 1 – Protected Circuits with High Short-Circuit Energy

acronyms.AFE: This is also called Active Front-End Converter
acronyms.PEI: Circuits in a DC Prosumer Electrical Installation
acronyms.BPBF: UPBF or UPF if PV source

normalized_text: DC Zone 1 – Protected Circuits with High Short-Circuit Energy Power distribution circuits, typically busbars, powered from sources connected in DC Zone 0 and protected by electromechanical circuit breakers or fuses are classified as DC Zone The fault energy in DC Zone 1 is limited by the OCPD according to its I²t rating, however, due to the relatively slow reaction of electromechanical circuit breakers and fuses, the residual fault energy and risk are still relatively high, also in consideration of the multiple sources. The main characteristics in DC Zone 1 are: • it is supplied by sources with high prospective short circuit current or high capacitance on the output; • high fault currents are possible; • multiple sources are possible, but they should all be connected to a main distribution board; • bidirectional power flow is possible; • high arc-flash incident energy is possible, requiring Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Category 2 according to NFPA 70. In the arc-flash assessment all connected sources need to be considered. These are typical characteristic values of DC Zone 1 (for information only): • arc-flash incident energy can exceed 8 cal/cm² (33.5 J/cm²); • typical time constants of the short circuit current are in the range of 1-5 ms; • typical fault let-through I²t is of the order x10⁶ A²s. Circuits in DC Zone 1 should be distributed only at short distances, for example inside a container, e.g. from the battery compartment to the electronics compartment, or in a dedicated room. The circuit protected by the fuse is classified in DC Zone 1. The circuit downstream the DC/DC converter is classified again as DC Zone 0 because the large DC-link capacitors can result in large fault current and energy.

DC Zone 1 is supplied by sources with high prospective short-circuit current or high capacitance on the output.
Bidirectional power flow is possible in DC Zone 1.
High arc-flash incident energy is possible in DC Zone 1 and requires Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Category 2 according to NFPA 70.
Circuits in DC Zone 1 should be distributed only at short distances, such as inside a container or in a dedicated room.
compacted_text: DC Zone 1 comprises power distribution circuits, typically busbars, supplied from DC Zone 0 sources and protected by electromechanical circuit breakers or fuses. Fault energy is limited by the OCPD according to its I²t rating, but residual fault energy and risk remain relatively high due to slow breaker/fuse response and multiple sources. Characteristics include high prospective short-circuit current or high output capacitance, possible high fault currents, multiple sources connected to a main distribution board, bidirectional power flow, and possible high arc-flash incident energy requiring PPE Category 2 per NFPA 70. All connected sources must be considered in arc-flash assessment. Typical values: arc-flash incident energy can exceed 8 cal/cm² (33.5 J/cm²), short-circuit current time constants are 1–5 ms, and fault let-through I²t is of the order of x10⁶ A²s. heading_path: Main > 9 Protection > 9.1.3 DC Zone 2.1 Current-limited circuits

acronyms.AFE: This is also called Active Front-End Converter
acronyms.PEI: Circuits in a DC Prosumer Electrical Installation

normalized_text: DC Zone 2.1 Current-limited circuits DC Zone 2.1 are power distribution circuits with multiple sources, where the available fault current and energy are limited by the sources, and protected by means of conventional OCPD (OverCurrent Protection Device), such as electromechanical circuit breakers or fuses. Note Photo-Voltaic sources are considered as DC Zone 2.1 even if no OCPD is installed. A circuit, or group of circuits, in a DC Prosumer Electrical Installation (COS PEI) is classified in DC Zone 2.1 if it meets the following requirements: • it is supplied only by current limited sources (e.g. PV panels) or current limited converters (e.g. buck converters) that by design cannot deliver a current significantly higher than its nominal current. It can be characterized as follows: short circuit current after 10ms is below 2 times the nominal current of the source (Isc @10ms < 2 In); • the overall energy stored in the capacitors connected to the circuits, including those integrated in converters and loads, shall not result in thermal damage to live conductors in case an faults. Rules intended to avoid this risk are detailed in Current/OS System Reference Document.. Note Photo-Voltaic sources are considered as DC Zone 2.1 (even if no OCPD is installed). In Current/OS, a DC Zone 2.1 circuit shall meet the following additional requirements • it is connected to other DC Zones according to the requirements in Table 5; • all sources are connected to a single distribution board. Note Possible additional requirements for loads and sources to ensure interoperability in DC Zone 2.1 are under consideration. Available fault energy in DC Zone 2.1 can be too low to trip electromechanical circuit breakers or below conventional fuses. Possible solutions are: • additional capacitors to increase the fault energy, without exceeding the 600J limit, or • an external relay to trigger the circuit breakers. This makes the design of the protection scheme a difficult task, requiring special skills. In particular, installations in DC Zone 2.1 are very difficult to maintain and upgrade. The main characteristics in DC Zone 2.1 are: • high overcurrents are possible depending on the number and characteristics of the sources; • bidirectional power flow is possible; • arc-flash incident energy can be high, depending on the number and , requiring special skills. In particular, installations in DC Zone 2.1 are very difficult to maintain and upgrade. The main characteristics in DC Zone 2.1 are: • high overcurrents are possible depending on the number and characteristics of the sources; • bidirectional power flow is possible; • arc-flash incident energy can be high, depending on the number and characteristics of the sources. Assessment is needed; • limited design flexibility, as multiple sources should be connected to the main distribution board; • selectivity requires a dedicated study considering the topology and the characteristics of the loads and the sources.

A circuit or group of circuits in a DC Prosumer Electrical Installation is classified as DC Zone 2.1 if it is supplied only by current-limited sources or converters that by design cannot deliver significantly higher than nominal current, with short-circuit current after 10 ms below 2 times nominal current (Isc @10ms < 2 In), and if capacitor energy, including capacitors in converters and loads, does not cause thermal damage to live conductors in a fault. In Current/OS, DC Zone 2.1 circuits must also be connected to other DC Zones per Table 5 and all sources must be connected to a single distribution

heading_path: Main > 9 Protection > 9.1.4 DC Zone 2.2 – Current limited circuits with fast protection

acronyms.AFE: This is also called Active Front-End Converter
acronyms.PEI: Circuits in a DC Prosumer Electrical Installation
acronyms.BPBF: UPBF or UPF if PV source

normalized_text: DC Zone 2.2 – Current limited circuits with fast protection Circuits in DC Zone 2.2 are characterized by low fault energy and current, as ensured by the design rules in this section and the use of fast OCPD (OverCurrent Protection Device), such as semiconductor hybrid circuit breakers (SCHCB). A circuit, or group of circuits, in a DC Prosumer Electrical Installation (COS PEI) is classified in DC Zone 2.2 if it meets following requirements: • it is supplied only by ◦ current limited sources (e.g. PV panels) or current limited converters (e.g. buck converters) that by design can not deliver a current significantly higher than its nominal current. It can be characterized as follows: short circuit current after 10 ms is below 2 times the nominal current of the source (Isc @10 ms < 2 In); ◦ a source without current limitation combined with an inductor that create the current limitation coordinated with the OCPD specified below; • sources and bidirectional loads are protected by OCPD having current limitation time not exceeding 1 ms (e.g. semiconductor hybrid circuit breakers SCHCB) (see section 9.1) with at least one device with current limitation time >10 µs; In Current/OS, DC Zone 2.2 circuits shall meet the following additional requirements • it is connected to other DC Zones according to the requirements in Table 5, • in case of fault, all power sources are automatically disconnected within 2 ms by intertripping means. The Current/OS Intertripping Wire (Section 8.4) is the recommended means; • the circuit includes a device with black-start master capability (Section 4.1.3); • conductors extending outdoor are shielded; • system earthing according to 5.1. Note Possible additional requirements for loads and sources to ensure interoperability in DC Zone 2.2 are under consideration. The main characteristics in DC Zone 2.2 are: • sources can be distributed in the installation; • bidirectional power flow is possible. • Protection against electric shock: ◦ If additional protection is needed, tripping time of protective device has to be checked according to disconneting time required by installation rules IEC 60364-4-41. Calculation is simpler compared to DC Zone 1 and 2.1, but information about cable lengths, inductances and capacitors may be needed. ◦ Disconnection of all supplies is safely ensured in case of fault. ◦ Selectivity performance needs to be assessed. • Protection against overcurrent: ◦ Calculation of short circuit current in the circuits is required. Contribution of (the number and the size) of the sources to the short-circuit current must be calculated. The sum of the limited short circuit currents does not exceed the OCPD maximum short circuit capacity. Let-through energy is lower than in DC Zone 2.1 due to the faster interruption but can be relatively high depending on the number and the size of the sources connected, ◦ The contribution of all the branches need to be considered in the evaluation of the overall short-circuit current and selectivity. • Protection against thermal effects: ◦ protective against thermal effect is enhanced by earth leakage protection and arc fault in circuits, ◦ arc flash incident energy is lower than in DC Zone 2.1 but can be still large in case many large sources are connected contribution of all the branches need to be considered in the evaluation of the overall short-circuit current and selectivity. • Protection against thermal effects: ◦ protective against thermal effect is enhanced by earth leakage protection and arc fault in circuits, ◦ arc flash incident energy is lower than in DC Zone 2.1 but can be still large in case many large sources are connected . • Protection against voltage disturbances and electromagnetic disturbances ◦ The voltage sag during a fault is reduced and sources disconnection is typically avoided but it can still cause stop and restart of some loads. ◦ Voltage ride-through requirements for loads need to be adapted to the duration of the voltage sag. ◦ A fault in load circuit supplied by a DC Zone 2.2 may affect the continuity of supply of other loads circuits. ◦ A fault in a source supplying a DC Zone 2.2 may cause the disconnection of other sources supplying the same zone.

compacted_text: DC Zone 2.2 circuits have low fault energy/current and use fast OCPD such as SCHCB. A circuit or group of circuits is classified as DC Zone 2.2 if supplied only by current-limited sources or current-limited converters that cannot deliver significantly higher than nominal current, with Isc @10 ms < 2 In, or by a non-current-limited source combined with an inductor coordinated with the specified OCPD. Sources and bidirectional loads are protected by OCPD with current limitation time not exceeding 1 ms, with at least one device >10 µs. Additional requirements: connected to other DC Zones per Table 5; all power sources automatically disconnected within 2 ms by intertripping; includes a black-start master capability device; outdoor conductors are shielded; system earthing per 5.1. Sources can be distributed and bidirectional power flow is possible. Short-circuit current must be calculated;

normalized_text: DC Zone 3 – Current limited circuits with ultra-fast protection Circuits in DC Zone 3 are fed by multiple sources and protected by fast semiconductor-based circuit breakers, ensuring very low fault current and energy. Sources do not need to be connected in a central panel and can be distributed in the installation. Loop and Mesh topologies are possible. A circuit, or group of circuits, in a DC Prosumer Electrical Installation (COS PEI) is classified in DC Zone 3 when it meets the following requirements: • short circuit and overload currents are limited to 2·In; • I²t total let through energy provided by the sum of all OCPD (OverCurrent Protection Device) is limited. Additional rules are detailed in Current/OS System Reference Document. • sources, bidirectional loads and connections with DC Zone 1, 2.1 and 2.2 circuits are protected by means of fast OCPD with time to current limitation not exceeding 10 µs and meeting the requirements in Section 9; Additional rules are detailed in Current/OS System Reference Document. NOTE As DC Zone 3 circuits are fed by multiple sources or bidirectional "prosumers" (e.g. battery systems), either from a central location or distributed, the power flow does not have a predetermined direction. The main characteristics in DC Zone 3 are: • sources can be distributed in the installation; • bidirectional power flow is possible. • Protection against electric shock: ◦ If additional protection is needed (80 mA RCD DC), time to clear dangerous voltage is low enough whatever the cable lengths, inductances and capacitors in the system up to U4 = 400 VDC (refer to chapter 9.4). ◦ Disconnection of all supplies according to IEC 60364-4-41 is safely ensured in case of fault. ◦ Selectivity is ensured. • Protection against overcurrent: ◦ Calculation of short circuit current in the circuits is not required. Contribution of (the number and the size) of the sources to the short-circuit current is limited thanks to the very low breaking time. ◦ Protection against overcurrent is ensured by the limitation of the supply thanks to the very fast (as specified above) interruptivity capability of each protection device. The associated limited short-circuit current is independent of the sum of the limited currents provided by the other feeders. ◦ Selectivity performance can be ensured either by the

Sources may be distributed; loop and mesh topologies are possible; power flow is bidirectional and has no predetermined direction. A circuit or group of circuits is classified as DC Zone 3 when short-circuit and overload currents are limited to 2·In, total let-through I²t energy from all OCPD is limited, and sources, bidirectional loads, and connections with DC Zone 1, 2.1, and 2.2 are protected by fast OCPD with time to current limitation not exceeding 10 µs. For electric shock protection, if additional protection is needed (80 mA RCD DC), dangerous voltage is cleared quickly up to U4 = 400 VDC; disconnection of all supplies per IEC 60364-4-41 is ensured in case of fault; selectivity is ensured.

acronyms.BPBF: UPBF or UPF if PV source

normalized_text: limited thanks to the very low breaking time. ◦ Protection against overcurrent is ensured by the limitation of the supply thanks to the very fast (as specified above) interruptivity capability of each protection device. The associated limited short-circuit current is independent of the sum of the limited currents provided by the other feeders. ◦ Selectivity performance can be ensured either by the devices current rating difference or the capacitances in the system. • Protection against thermal effects: ◦ protection against thermal effect is enhanced by earth leakage protection and arc fault in circuits, ◦ arc-flash incident energy is always lower than in 1.2 cal/cm² (5 J/cm²). • Protection against voltage disturbances and electromagnetic disturbances ◦ The voltage sag during a fault is very short, except in some extreme cases with cables many kilometers long. Sources disconnection is typically avoided. And it does not cause stop and restart of some loads. ◦ The loads typical voltage ride-through capability is generally enough to avoid any disconnection. An example of DC Zone 3 system is. All sources are connected to the bus via a fast protection device, for example an SCOB. DC Zone 3 offers the following advantages: - Ease of design: no short circuit calculation needed at system level. Possibility to validate the protection coordination via real testing, - Design flexibility: circuits can be distributed over long distances, radial and meshed topologies can be easily used, sources do not need to be connected to the main panel and can be distributed on the bus. - Thermal and electrodynamic stresses on the conductors in case of fault are low. - Conductor's cross section can be optimized according to the rated current and the voltage drop. - Additional protections can be achieved line to earth according to IEC 60479 up to 400 VDC with TN-S earthing (Sect. 7.1), because of the RCD protection and fast disconnection of all power sources, provided these sources are connected to earth via PCR diode networks. - Risk assessment for operation in vicinity of live circuit is simplified, high rated PPE are not necessary as incident energy remains below 1.2 cal/cm². - Diode and PCR arrangement for earthing (Sect. 7.6) is easier. - Devices can be designed for an OVC one level lower compared to those in DC Zone 0 and 1. - Improved voltage stability and droop control because of the low inrush current. - Simplified coordination of protection and selectivity. 9.1.6 DC Zone 4 – Single-source current limited circuits with ultra-fast protection Circuits in DC Zone 4 are fed by a single source and protected by fast semiconductor-based circuit breakers, ensuring very low fault current and energy. Circuits in a DC Prosumer Electrical Installation (PEI) meeting the requirements of DC Zone 3 and fed from a single source are classified as DC Zone 4. DC Zone 4 shall contain only one source therefore power flow is unidirectional. Regenerative loads shall not be connected to DC zone 4. Loads shall be compatible with DC Zone 4 operations. In particular, no capacitors can face the system, which means that diodes are required in loads when a capacitive load is used. Design and installation rules are the same as in DC Zone 3, with no need for intertripping means. The benefits in DC Zone 4 are at least equal to DC Loads shall be compatible with DC Zone 4 operations. In particular, no capacitors can face the system, which means that diodes are required in loads when a capacitive load is used. Design and installation rules are the same as in DC Zone 3, with no need for intertripping means. The benefits in DC Zone 4 are at least equal to DC Zone 3, in particular: - Ultra-limited fault currents on the DC zone 4 circuit

heading_path: Main > 9 Protection > 9.2.1 Specific requirements for DC Zone 2.2

acronyms.AFE: This is also called Active Front-End Converter
acronyms.PEI: Circuits in a DC Prosumer Electrical Installation
acronyms.BPBF: UPBF or UPF if PV source

normalized_text: Specific requirements for DC Zone 2.2 Circuits in DC Zone 2.2 are protected by semiconductor hybrid circuit breakers (SCHCB) according to the (future) IEC 60947-10 standard meeting the following requirements: - SCHCB’s or SCCB’s current limitation time shall not exceed 1 ms. - SCHCB’s or SCCB’s critical rate of rise of the current (di/dt)c shall not be lower than the the maximum possible rate of rise of the fault current. In case the SSCB does not meet this requirements, the rate of rise of the current can be reduced by means of an additional inductor, - SCHCB’s or SCCB’s short-circuit breaking capacity Icu shall not be lower than the maximum available short-circuit current in the circuit, - SCHCB’s or SCCB’s maximum switchable inductance Lmax Shall not be lower than the maximum fault inductance in the circuit, - SCHCB’s or SCCB’s maximum switching overvoltage Usw shall be lower than U5. The fault current analysis in DC Zone 2.2 shall consider the contribution of all components in the circuits, including cables and also all capacitors included in sources and loads. In case distributed sources are present in the cell, intertripping feature shall be implemented for simultaneous disconnection as per 5.7 paragraph. The Current/OS Intertripping Wire or a different intertripping means can be used. Protection devices interconnecting two DC Zone 2.2 cells, e.g. in ring / meshed grids, shall provide bidirectional protection. Thanks to these, Copper wires having cross section equal to or larger than 1.5 mm2 are protected against short circuit current in DC Zone 2.2 because of the limitation of the let-through energy..
statements: Specific requirements for DC Zone 2.2 circuits are protected by semiconductor hybrid circuit breakers (SCHCB) according to the (future) IEC 60947-10 standard.
The current limitation time of an SCHCB or SCCB shall not exceed 1 ms.
The critical rate of rise of the current (di/dt)c of an SCHCB or SCCB shall not be lower than the maximum possible rate of rise of the fault current.
The short-circuit breaking capacity Icu of an SCHCB or SCCB shall not be lower than the maximum available short-circuit current in the circuit.
The maximum switchable inductance Lmax of an SCHCB or SCCB shall not be lower than the maximum fault inductance in the circuit.
The fault current analysis in DC Zone 2.2 shall consider the contribution of all components in the circuits, including cables and all capacitors included in sources and loads.
Protection devices interconnecting two DC Zone 2.2 cells shall provide bidirectional protection.
compacted_text: DC Zone 2.2 circuits are protected by SCHCBs according to future IEC 60947-10. Current limitation time shall not exceed 1 ms; critical di/dt shall not be lower than the maximum possible fault-current rise; if not met, an additional inductor can reduce the rise rate. Short-circuit breaking capacity Icu shall not be lower than the maximum available short-circuit current; maximum switchable inductance Lmax shall not be lower than the maximum fault inductance; maximum switching overvoltage Usw shall be lower than U5. Fault current analysis shall include all components, including cables and capacitors in sources and loads. If distributed sources are present, intertripping shall be implemented for simultaneous disconnection; Current/OS Intertripping Wire or another means can be used. Devices interconnecting two DC Zone 2.2 cells in ring/meshed grids shall provide bidirectional protection.

acronyms.AFE: This is also called Active Front-End Converter
acronyms.PEI: Circuits in a DC Prosumer Electrical Installation

normalized_text: Specific requirements for DC Zone 3 and 4 Circuits in DC Zone 3 and 4 are protected by semiconductor circuit breakers according to the (future) IEC 60947-10 standard meeting the following requirements: - SCCB’s current limitation time shall not exceed 10 µs, - SCCB’s maximum switching overvoltage Usw shall be lower than U5, - SCCB’s critical rate of rise of the current (di/dt)c shall not be lower than the maximum possible rate of rise of the fault current, - SCCB’s maximum switchable inductance Lmax shall not be lower than the maximum fault inductance in the circuit. NOTE: the fault analysis is dramatically simplified due to the ultrafast opening time, as only the capacitors in the closest source or load are supposed to contribute to the faults and shall be considered. All sources in DC Zone 3 cells shall have intertripping feature (the Current/OS Intertripping Wire) according to 5.7. Protection devices interconnecting two DC Zone 3 cells, e.g. in ring / meshed grids, shall provide bidirectional protection. Protection devices shall implement short-circuit protection. The tripping devices shall trip at a current not exceeding 2x the nominal current of the OCPD (OverCurrent Protection Device) (e.g. a 16 A OCPD shall break the current before it reaches 32 A). The opening time till current limitation shall not exceed 10 µs NOTE: 2x nominal current threshold is chosen as a requirement for protection devices. This is a compromise between oversizing of the switching components in protection devices and the load over-current allowed. The contribution of devices in a cell shall meet the fault energy criteria of the DC Zone (as per 0 for DC Zone 3, and 9.1.6 for DC Zone 4), As a consequence, conductors equal to or bigger than 1.5 mm² copper conductor is protected against short circuit current because of limitation of the let through energy..

heading_path: Main > General > General
normalized_text: As global energy demands surge – driven by the rapid expansion of data centers and AI applications – power distribution systems must evolve. Symmetrical two-wire Direct Current (DC) systems offer a compelling alternative to traditional three-wire configurations, delivering superior energy efficiency, simplified installation, and enhanced reliability. This whitepaper outlines the advantages of symmetrical DC systems and their role in enabling scalable, sustainable, and simple digital infrastructure. 1 The Growing Challenge The global data center market is projected to grow from $527 billion in 2025 to $739 billion by 2030, representing a compounded annual growth rate (CAGR) of 6.98%. This growth is driven by increasing demand for cloud computing, AI workloads, and digital services across industries. Key challenges include: • Energy Efficiency: Reducing power loss is essential to maximizing computing output per watt. • Reliability: Complex AC/DC conversion chains and numerous components increase the risk of failure. • Installation Complexity: Frequent AC/DC conversions and three-wire systems require more components, resources, and labor. • Operational Control: Managing multiple voltage levels complicates system stability and monitoring.

normalized_text: This report analyses three key technologies of the electricity grid, each characterized by different levels of maturity and deployment. A summary of the main findings for each technology is reported below. HVDC Components HVDC systems have a fundamental role in the network infrastructure for two reasons: — HVDC systems contribute to the smooth integration of new renewable sources and the support to an efficient operation of a decarbonized grid. — The HVDC link can act as a controlled transfer channel when the AC system is experiencing issues (e.g., voltage instability, frequency deviations or faults) by maintaining a constant power flow, preventing unintended power oscillations that could exacerbate AC system instability (e.g., during faults or load imbalances) and providing inertia and damping by decoupling the DC and AC systems, allowing the AC grid time to recover. The study includes the main physical components of HVDC systems, i.e., HVDC converters, DC circuit breakers and cables. Most HVDC technologies have reached operational maturity (TRL 9), while emerging solutions like High Temperature Superconductor (HTS) cables and ultra-high-voltage (UHV) systems require further validation. Regarding the costs, HVDC becomes cost-competitive with AC especially at distances greater than 300 miles (483 km), due to lower losses and reduced infrastructure. The biggest part of a HVDC project cost are for materials (57%) and substations (26%). When it comes to the European position in the global market, Europe holds ~30% of the global HVDC converter station market, with Germany (20%) and the UK (15%) leading investments. Key European manufacturers in HVDC cables include NKT, Nexans, Sükabel, and Prysmian. In 2024, Europe HVDC Cables Market was valued at USD 3.8 Billion and the European circuit breaker market at USD 4.2billion. Horizon Europe is funding 3 projects between 2024-2028 under the topic HVAC, HVDC and High-Power cable systems with a total grant of 20.29 MEuros. These focus on advanced cable systems, predictive models, and grid unification. Privately, large transmission operators are also investing in HVDC systems. Amongst them TennetT (Dutch-German TSO) and the UK’s National Grid are leading large-scale tenders (€30 billion and £59 billion, respectively) for HVDC infrastructure. The European position is strong in regional

normalized_text: advanced cable systems, predictive models, and grid unification. Privately, large transmission operators are also investing in HVDC systems. Amongst them TennetT (Dutch-German TSO) and the UK’s National Grid are leading large-scale tenders (€30 billion and £59 billion, respectively) for HVDC infrastructure. The European position is strong in regional projects and R&D but it lags China in UHVDC innovation and supply chain scale. Moreover, there are still many challenges due to the high upfront costs with long payback periods material shortages in Europe especially for copper, aluminium and semiconductors, which are exacerbated by rising demand and finally the long lead times (2-3 years for cables and transformers) due to limited manufacturing capacity. Switchgear Switchgear is a crucial component for the grid, as it is used to control, protect and isolate electrical equipment. It protects critical electrical equipment by switching on and off. The main drivers for the switchgear market are: the expansion of the distribution and transmission grids; the upgrade of ageing grid infrastructure; modernization of the grid; growth of digital technologies; increase in RES to be integrated within the grid. Switchgears are categorised depending on their voltage level, their insulation, their installation and the end-user application. Switchgears are part of the grid for decades and the Technology Readiness Level is TRL 9 for typical switchgear products. However, there are efforts to make switchgears more environmentally friendly with insulation materials that have a lower CO2 footprint. For such switchgears, research is ongoing, thus the technology readiness level is lower. In this report, we refer to the total production of switchgears within the EU and beyond, and we also list the main materials for switchgear manufacturing. We also examine the current switchgear market and its growth perspective for the future. In general, the market is growing and this is expected to continue for the coming years. EU manufacturers are among the main players for switchgears manufacturing, holding a significant share in the overall switchgear market. Grid-Enhancing Technologies Grid-Enhancing Technologies (GETs) encompass a range of hardware and software solutions designed to increase the capacity, efficiency and reliability of the existing electricity grid infrastructure. This report focuses on three of the most promising GETs: — Dynamic Line Rating (DLR): determines the actual thermal capacity of transmission lines using real-time and forecasted environmental data. — Advanced Power Flow Control (APFC): enables the redirection of electrical power between transmission lines, helping to reduce congestions and eliminate bottlenecks. — Topology Optimization (TO): a software-based solution that reconfigures the grid layout using existing network switches to improve system reliability and efficiency. The Technological Readiness Level varies across these GETs, generally ranging from 6 to 9. Several real-word operational projects have already been deployed (in particular for DLR) while others remain at the prototype or demonstration stage, with a mature technological market yet to emerge, especially for TO. Preliminary analyses indicate that GETs have significant potential to reduce or defer costly network expansion investments, with global estimates of up to € 700 Bn in avoided infrastructure costs. In addition, GETs have already been deployed (in particular for DLR) while others remain at the prototype or demonstration stage, with a mature technological market yet to emerge, especially for TO. Preliminary analyses indicate that GETs have significant potential to reduce or defer costly network expansion investments, with global estimates of up to € 700 Bn in avoided infrastructure costs. In addition, GETs could alleviate some of the supply chain issues related to the growing demand for materials used in network assets, particularly copper, aluminium and steel. The EU is a global leader in terms of GET-related patenting, scientific publications and public R&D funding. European companies also hold strong market positions as providers of GET solutions.

normalized_text: Nevertheless, several challenges must be addressed to fully unlock the potential of GETs. These include incompatibilities of GETs with current grid planning methodologies, the lack of proper incentive mechanisms for grid innovation and the difficulties of scaling up initial pilot projects into broader deployment. SWOT Analysis Table 1. CETO SWOT analysis for the competitiveness of HVDC systems Strengths — HVDC is economically viable for long-distance transmission — HVDC lines require fewer conductors and narrower routes. This means less land is used, less visual impact, and lower costs for land acquisition Weaknesses — Patent leadership by China (State Grid, CSG) and the U.S. (General Electric) highlights EU reliance on non-European innovators — China leads the way in ultra-high-voltage direct current (UHVDC) technology — Shortage of skilled labour for offshore HVDC installations Opportunities — The TYNDP 2022 contains transmission projects with over 43,000 km of potential additional cables and lines, of which 25,000 km (58 %) are DC — Innovative solutions with improved raw material use are emerging, including higher capacity cables and superconducting cables for long-range bulk transmission of power without energy losses. In addition, raw material use for related infrastructure would be significantly lower. Threats — Critical raw materials needed for HVDCs constitute scarce resources with sovereignty risks for Europe — China has emerged as a global leader in HVDC development and can sidestep the supply constraints facing other regions. Source: JRC 2025 Table 2. CETO SWOT analysis for the competitiveness of Switchgear Strengths — EU companies hold an important market share in switchgears — Switchgears have a TRL 9 for typical switchgears and TRL 7 – 8 for switchgears with advanced technologies, like SF6-free insulation Weaknesses — Strong presence of non-EU manufacturers in the market, with China leading the switchgears exports at worldwide level — Non uniform growth among EU Member States regarding switchgear production Opportunities — The switchgear market is growing and is expected to grow in the following years at worldwide level. — Opportunities can be: — Expansion of the grid — Upgrade of ageing infrastructure — Grid modernization — Increasing share of RES — Growth of digital technologies for the grid Threats — Reliance on raw materials for switchgears imported from third countries Source — The switchgear market is growing and is expected to grow in the following years at worldwide level. — Opportunities can be: — Expansion of the grid — Upgrade of ageing infrastructure — Grid modernization — Increasing share of RES — Growth of digital technologies for the grid Threats — Reliance on raw materials for switchgears imported from third countries Source : JRC 2025 Table 3. normalized_text: Technology Status and Development Trends 2.1.1. Technology Overview In its basic structure, a HVDC system includes: - Circuit breakers: HVDC circuit breaker is a switching device that interrupts the flow of normal and abnormal DC. DC breakers are more complex and expensive that the AC ones due to the absence of zero crossing and the need of fast electronic switches for high currents. (ChinGlobal, 2025) - HVDC converters, including AC/DC and DC/AC converters and equipment for reactive power support and filtering. The AC/DC and DC/AC converters can generally use two different topologies: Line Commutated Converters (LCC), a well-established technology relying on thyristors, and Voltage Source Converters (VSC), which are more recent and provide greater controllability. VSCs can generate Alternating Current (AC) voltages without the need to rely on an AC system. This allows for independent rapid control of both active and reactive power and black start capability. VSCs maintain a constant polarity of the Direct Current (DC) voltage and control the DC current flow. This ability enables the application of cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE) cables. - HVDC conductors, which can either be onshore (overhead or underground) or offshore (mainly submarine cables). A high-voltage direct current (HVDC) electric power transmission system uses direct current (DC) for the transmission of electrical power instead of the more common alternating current (AC). It allows efficient electricity transmission across long distances and supports offshore wind farm integration, particularly for large, distant offshore farms where underwater AC cabling is not feasible. Sub-technologies of HVDC cables include: - HVDC Mass Impregnated (MI) cables are a very consolidated and traditional technology mainly deployed for subsea applications. Initially, MI cables were used with LCCs. Currently, this technology is mainly deployed for Extra High Voltage (EHV) DC subsea applications. The latest developments in MI cables allow voltages of up to 600 kV. - Extruded cables are insulated using XLPE as an alternative to oil or MI. Thermal properties of XLPE allow a continuous maximum conductor temperature of 90°C and a maximum short circuit temperature of 250°C and, therefore, higher transmission capacity per cable compared to other technologies. - High Temperature Superconductors Cables: These conductors are placed in a pipe with vacuum (cryogen cables are insulated using XLPE as an alternative to oil or MI. Thermal properties of XLPE allow a continuous maximum conductor temperature of 90°C and a maximum short circuit temperature of 250°C and, therefore, higher transmission capacity per cable compared to other technologies. - High Temperature Superconductors Cables: These conductors are placed in a pipe with vacuum (cryogen ) which thermally isolates the superconductor from the remaining environment. They carry five times the current of a conventional cable system with the same outer dimensions, and they do not emit any heat to the environment.

normalized_text: 0 km. Voltage Source Converters (VSC) have been developed more recently but they are being utilised in most of the new HVDC projects as they allow rapid control of active and reactive power. These converters generally achieve a TRL of 8-9, with the exception of DC/DC converter which are currently only being validated in lab (TRL 4). HVDC transmission has nowadays reached a significant level of maturity. As indicated in the technology factsheets by ENTSO-E, the bulk of the HVDC-related technologies have already been proven in the operational environment of actual system (TRL 9). IEA indicates that the HVDC power transmission has reached proof of stability. In terms of conductors, Mass Impregnated (MI) cables represent a very consolidated and traditional technology for HVDC system, used for both on-shore underground connections and off-shore applications (TRL 9). Crosslinked Polyethylene (XLPE) cables, i.e. conductors with extruded insulation, are seeing an increased diffusion as they can operate at a wide range of temperatures and are particularly resistant to corrosion and vibrations. XLPE cables operating at 320kV are a very mature technology (TRL 9) while their application at 525kV is still being validated (TRL 5) and their use at 600 kV is at an experimental stage (TRL 3). Lastly, High Temperature superconductor (HTS) cables have a TRL of 5-6. The literature provides a TRL of 5 as testing on integrated systems is still limited, but the recent demonstration of the FP7 funded Best Paths project, with a range of at least 6. IEA is in line here with a TRL of 6-7 (precommercial demonstration). 2.1.3. Installed capacity The TYNDP 2022 contains transmission projects with over 43,000 km of potential additional cables and lines, of which 25,000 km (58 %) are DC. Moreover, HVDC projects, which are currently in the planning or construction phase, are mainly VSC-based. Approximately 30 (VSC-based) HVDC projects are already operational in Europe below on the left. A projection to 2050 shows a possible total installed capacity in the magnitude of 1 TW, assuming the growth rate remains similar to currently experienced growth rates (up to 2030).

Global markets and growth prospects The leading producer of converter stations is Hitachi Energy (formerly ABB) in Switzerland, followed by Siemens (Germany), General Electric (United States), Mitsubishi Electric (Japan), NR Electric and C-EPRI Electric Power Engineering (China) and Bharat Heavy Electricals Limited (India). Europe has approximately 30% of the global market share in the HVDC Converter Station Industry, with Germany leading with 20% of this share and investments exceeding the b€1, followed by UK with 15% and budgets of over b€2 (Insights, Global Growth, 2024a) Globally, the highest market share in HVDC converters is in Asia Pacific (approx. 35%), followed by Europe (~30%) and North America (~22%). In terms of cables, the main European companies include NKT in Denmark, Nexans in France, Südkabel in Germany and Prysmian in Italy. Outside Europe, the most relevant companies are Sumitomo in Japan, General Cable in the United States, NBO and ZTT in China and LS Cable in Korea. Europe HVDC Cables Market was valued at USD 3.8 billion in 2024 and is anticipated to grow at a CAGR of 5.06% from 2025 to 2033, (Market Data Forecast, 2024) Eurone circuit breaker market was valued at USD 4.2 billion in 2024 and is estimated to grow at a CAGR 5.8% from 2025 to 2034 due to the increasing investments in renewable energy infrastructure, urbanization, and the modernization of aging electrical grids. Rising electricity demand across residential, commercial, and industrial sectors is propelling the adoption of advanced circuit breaker technologies, showing a CAGR of 5.8% in the period 2025-2034. The shift towards green energy solutions, including solar and wind power, requires reliable grid integration and fault management, boosting the demand for circuit breakers. (Global Market Insights, 2024b) The global HVDC Circuit Breaker market size was estimated at USD 874 million in 2023 and is projected to reach USD 1761.74 million by 2034. The shift towards green energy solutions, including solar and wind power, requires reliable grid integration and fault management, boosting the demand for circuit breakers. (Global Market Insights, 2024b) The global HVDC Circuit Breaker market size was estimated at USD 874 million in 2023 and is projected to reach USD 1761.74 million by 2032, exhibiting a CAGR of 8.10% during the forecast period. (Semiconductor Insight, 2024) The HVDC Transmission Market in general was valued at USD 12.98 Billion in 2024, and is expected to reach USD 19.05 Billion by 2030, rising at a CAGR of 6.44%. (MENA FN, 2025) Key Market Challenges The HVDC transmission market faces a major hurdle in the form of substantial upfront capital expenditure while the payback periods are long. Establishing HVDC infrastructure requires costly equipment like converter stations, specialized cables, and advanced control technologies. The total expense often reaches billions of dollars, which limits accessibility for financially constrained entities and restricts adoption to well-funded or state-backed initiatives. Additionally, extended project timelines and long-term return on investment deter short-term-focused investors.

The gas based insulation segments are experiencing growth due to their usability. They are suitable for areas with limited space or exposed to high level of pollution. In addition their low running cost and their safety characteristics make them attractive for several applications (Ins ightAceAnalytics, 2025). Categorisation according to installation: For indoor and outdoor installed switchgears, we can have Gas Insulated high voltage switchgear (GIS), air-insulated switchgear (AIS) and other types of switchgears. When the voltage is over 66 kV, outdoor switchgears are used. Due to the large spacing between the conductor and the insulator, installation costs are increased. Outdoor switchgears function efficiently under difficult climatic conditions. ears. When the voltage is over 66 kV, outdoor switchgears are used. Due to the large spacing between the conductor and the insulator, installation costs are increased. Outdoor switchgears function efficiently under difficult climatic conditions. For voltage below 66 kV, indoor switchgears are used, entailing an enclosed metal casing, thus ensuring simple maintenance and high durability. Categorisation according to end-user: Different end-user categories for switchgears can be: transmission and distribution utilities; commercial and residential; industrial. Switchgears for transmission and distribution utilities are very popular under this category. 3.1.2. Technology Readiness Level Switchgears have been present in the global market for decades, thus indicating a high technology readiness level. Specifically for HV switchgears, they have been in use for more than 50 years and have gone through continuous technological modifications and improvements. For Gas insulated switchgears, the most common material to be used is sulphur hexafluoride (SF6), which has superior dielectric and insulating properties with respect to solid and liquid insulation. However, it is important that switchgears are up to date with the latest technological advancements in order to accommodate the needs of the modern grid and integrate the increasing amount of energy by RES (Subramaniam, Sahoo, Manohar, Raman, & Panda, 2021). New technologies appear on the market and therefore, devices with gases as their dielectric, CO2, O2, air, can have an improved CO2 footprint with respect to SF6. A couple of examples of emerging new technologies for switchgears are: the arc quenching using a vacuum chamber isolated with gas of natural origin and the direct arc quenching in insulating gas, which resembles the SF6 technology. These technologies are under development. As for the technology readiness level for SF6 switchgears, this is a circuit breaker technology that has been developed over the last 50 years and has been used for several applications. Therefore, this technology has been greatly advanced and advancements have also been made to limit the SF6-gas emissions. The technology is considered reliable. On the other hand, SF6-free technology is planned to be introduced in the following years and still needs further improvement. For this reason, research and development activities can contribute to improve such technologies. In general, the following classification can be made, as shown in Table 6.

Development of switchgears: Switchgears are becoming smarter with the inclusion of solutions like the IoT and artificial intelligence tools. Due to such solutions, any faults are predicted and corrective actions can be taken. In addition, real-time data and visibility can increase system efficiency. With smart grid applications being a reality, switchgears are required to have advanced monitoring and control units along with digital solutions. Thus, switchgears with advanced features is a new trend. Many medium voltage switchgears are ready for usage in the smart grid and they are more reliable and safe. In addition, switchgears enable automated operations and energy management. Furthermore, switching in vacuum is well-proven in medium voltage. For HV vacuum breakers, the traditional design has gas as insulation. However, pure F-gas (C4-FN and C5-FK) cannot be used in switchgear, with C5-FK being better compatible with MV applications than with HV applications (René Smeets, 2021). While the low voltage switchgears dominate the market, medium voltage switchgears are expected to have an increase. With respect to switchgears insulation, oil and vacuum insulation are greatly developed, as they compose the 54% of market share for 2022. Such switchgears can accommodate increasing renewable energy sources and they can accommodate electric vehicles in an efficient way. The gas segment as insulation material is also experiencing growth with the developing countries being the most important driver. Gas insulation allows for an efficient monitoring and control of the electricity network. It is used for limited space areas, as it can occupy 10% of the space required by an air insulated switchgear and it can offer great performance and power handling capacity. Regarding the installation of switchgears, outdoor switchgears are the most popular segment, due to their extensive utilization in transmission lines and distribution substations (imarc, 2024). With respect to the type of end use, the industrial usage of switchgears holds the largest market share, as there is a growing demand for industries, like manufacturing, oil and gas, mining and power generation to ensure the correct functionality of heavy equipment (imarc, 2024).
