normalized_text: type of end use, the industrial usage of switchgears holds the largest market share, as there is a growing demand for industries, like manufacturing, oil and gas, mining and power generation to ensure the correct functionality of heavy equipment (imarc, 2024). Indeed, the expansion of industrial facilities and automation technologies have created an increased demand for sophisticated switchgears, ensuring secure power supply and an advanced operational safety. Smart switchgears can enable monitoring capabilities and enhanced automation services, facilitating industrial applications. Apart from the industrial sector, switchgears are experiencing growth also in the commercial sector. Maintenance or replacement of switchgears: For low voltage switchgears, a method applied is to wait until the equipment has failed in order to repair or replace it. This method is suitable for this category of switchgears, especially when the maintenance cost exceeds the cost of the equipment itself. However, for MV and HV switchgears, this is often not a suitable method to follow, where preventive maintenance is usually followed. According to it, maintenance is performed on a fixed date and time irrespective of the switchgear status. Another type of maintenance is the condition-based maintenance, performed according to the conditions of the switchgear, as the name implies. This is done mainly by monitoring critical and HV switchgears, this is often not a suitable method to follow, where preventive maintenance is usually followed. According to it, maintenance is performed on a fixed date and time irrespective of the switchgear status. Another type of maintenance is the condition-based maintenance, performed according to the conditions of the switchgear, as the name implies. This is done mainly by monitoring critical indicators and by performing maintenance actions when such indicators show decreasing performance (Subramaniam, Sahoo, Manohar, Raman, & Panda, 2021). Standards for switchgears: The standard setting guidelines for manufacturers and users of switchgears is mainly the IEC 62271 standards family. IEC 62271-1 states the recommendations for actions to be taken after a failure event. The IEC 62271-4 is describing commissioning, service, maintenance and equipment end-of-life for gas insulated switchgears (Subramaniam, Sahoo, Manohar, Raman, & Panda, 2021). 3.1.3. Installed energy capacity and production generation The installed capacity of switchgears in the EU and at worldwide level is evaluated based on grid infrastructure rather than a single value. The EU has an advanced electrical grid, which supports high-voltage transmission (400 kV, 220 kV) and medium/ low-voltage distribution (24 kV, 11 kV, 400 V). Specifically for transmission level switchgears, the 400 kV Gas-Insulated Switchgear installations alone are above 100,000 MVA in breaking capacity. Some major projects, like Nordlink, IFA2 interconnectors, use 63 kA-rated switchgears with total capacity of 1.4 GW per interconnector. For distribution-level switchgears (24 kV – 40.5 kV), there are millions of MV switchgear panels to support the grid. Cumulative capacity in the EU is evaluated at hundreds of gigawatts (GW) in installed load capacity. With respect to the production in the EU, shows the total production value in the EU and the top producer countries in million of euros. The total production shows an increase in the last years, with the 2024 value set at 3581 million euros. Italy and Germany are the top producers in the EU. shows the top EU producers including Italy and Germany for the last decade.

normalized_text: Market from end user point of view: The commercial and residential segment of switchgears is an important part of the switchgears market and is expected to grow with respect to its CAGR in the future years. Especially for the residential sector, the rising urban population together with the home automation needs, the growing digitalization and the penetration of electric vehicles, render switchgears a valuable component for modern electrical distribution and protection systems. On the other hand, transmission and distribution utilities, which is the largest end-user segment, are expected to grow at the highest CAR. 3.3.1. Global markets and growth prospects Geographical characteristics of the switchgears market: Asia Pacific: Asia Pacific is the fastest-growing region in terms of switchgear market, expected to grow at 7% until 2029, with leading countries being China, India, South Korea and Japan. Other countries pushing forward the switchgear market and in general the industry growth are Australia and the Southeast Asian countries. It has been estimated that Asia Pacific dominated the market with a share of 43.61% in 2019 (Insights G. M., 2024). For the near future years to come, it is expected that the Asia Pacific region experiences the maximum CAGR, 8.1%. Europe: The region that comes second in the list regarding the switchgears market (2024 data) is western Europe. In general, for the period 2019 – 2024, the switchgear market in Europe has experienced a growth of around 6%. Smart grid applications and solutions to mitigate CO2 emissions, like SF6-free for switchgears are common solutions in Europe. The growth in the European switchgear market is also due to several regulations for the integration of renewable energy sources along with the management of existing long-standing transmission and distribution networks. Countries playing a key role are among others, Germany, Italy, Spain, the UK and France. North America: North America is also expected to experience a growth in the switchgear market, mainly due to the increased investments in renewable energy sources and their integration. The region is anticipated to significantly contribute to the global market. with main countries the USA and Canada. During 2024, the share of the north American switchgear market has been approximately 23%, where industries like oil and gas, chemical and power generation sectors are dominating the field. Latin America: Likewise the other regions, Latin America also experiences growth in the switchgear market The region is anticipated to significantly contribute to the global market. with main countries the USA and Canada. During 2024, the share of the north American switchgear market has been approximately 23%, where industries like oil and gas, chemical and power generation sectors are dominating the field. Latin America: Likewise the other regions, Latin America also experiences growth in the switchgear market , mainly driven by the increasing integration of renewable energy sources, which are in turn favoured by environmental conditions, like solar and wind. The region is experiencing investments by foreign investors for the transmission network expansion and the growth in infrastructure development for utilities, mining, oil and gas and construction. Countries that play a key role in the switchgears market are for example Brazil, Chile and Mexico. Middle East and Africa: Middle East and Africa is also a region with great potential for the expansion of switchgears market, with the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (KSA), Qatar, the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Kuwait anticipated to be the major drivers for switchgears expansion, as these countries are making investments for the grid enhancements (InsightAceAnalytics, 2025). Especially for Saudi Arabia, the country has made significant investments to expand infrastructure, including switchgears, and upgrade its grid. However, the Middle East and Latin America are expected to hold a small share in the global market.

normalized_text: The rapid growth of renewable energy generation and the electrification of key sectors such as transportation and heating are fundamentally transforming energy production, transmission, and consumption across the grid. These shifts are placing mounting pressure on existing transmission and distribution infrastructure, prompting the urgent need for substantial investments in new assets and upgrades to current systems. As a cost-effective and scalable alternative, Grid-Enhancing Technologies (GETs) offer innovative solutions to improve the efficiency, intelligence, and resilience of the existing grid. This report examines three of the most promising GETs: Dynamic Line Rating (DLR), Advanced Power Flow Control (APFC), and Topology Optimization (TO) software, assessing their potential to optimize grid performance and support the energy transition.
statements: The growth of renewable energy generation and electrification of transportation and heating are transforming energy production, transmission, and consumption across the grid.
These shifts are increasing pressure on existing transmission and distribution infrastructure, creating an urgent need for substantial investments in new assets and upgrades.
Grid-Enhancing Technologies (GETs) provide cost-effective and scalable solutions to improve the efficiency, intelligence, and resilience of the existing grid.
compacted_text: Rapid growth of renewable energy generation and electrification of transportation and heating are transforming energy production, transmission, and consumption across the grid, placing pressure on existing transmission and distribution infrastructure and creating a need for substantial investments in new assets and upgrades. Grid-Enhancing Technologies (GETs) are presented as a cost-effective, scalable alternative to improve the efficiency, intelligence, and resilience of the existing grid. The report examines Dynamic Line Rating (DLR), Advanced Power Flow Control (APFC), and Topology Optimization (TO) software.

normalized_text: Value Chain Analysis The deployment of GETs has significant potential to increase the efficiency and capacity of the European electricity network while reducing costs. If adopted swiftly, GETs could boost overall network capacity by 10–20% by 2030 and by 20–40% by 2040, according to recent estimates. In terms of costs the grid investments required to achieve decarbonisation targets are estimated at €1,000 Bn each for both transmission and distribution networks by 2040. However, the integration of GETs could offer substantial cost savings. Projections estimates that GETs could reduce the need for physical network expansion by as much as 35%, translating into approximately €700 Bn in avoided infrastructure investments by 2040. This evaluation does not account for the deployment costs of the GETs which, however, are estimated to be cheaper than their equivalent conventional reinforcements. In terms of value chain, the large-scale deployments of GETs would reduce the growing demand of material for network assets, such as aluminium and copper for cables and steel for transformers, alleviating the associated supply chain criticalities. Despite these promising advantages, several challenges must be addressed to enable widespread adoption of GETs. One significant obstacle is the current lack of incentives for choosing non-CAPEX-intensive solutions, which are often deprioritized in favour of traditional capital expenditure approaches. Additionally, there is insufficient recognition and reward for innovation within the regulatory frameworks governing the electricity sector. Another critical issue is the incompatibility of GET with the planning methodologies typically used by network operators, which often do not account for the flexibility and performance capabilities that GET can provide. The operators in some cases can be reluctant to adopt relatively untested technology in place of well-established solutions. Finally, innovative projects frequently face a "death-by-pilot" scenario, where technologies are tested in isolated pilot projects but fail to achieve broader deployment due to systemic barriers.

GETs face barriers to broader deployment because innovative projects can experience a "death-by-pilot" scenario.
compacted_text: GETs could increase European electricity network capacity by 10–20% by 2030 and 20–40% by 2040. Grid investments for decarbonisation are estimated at €1,000 Bn each for transmission and distribution by 2040, while GETs could reduce physical network expansion by up to 35%, avoiding about €700 Bn in infrastructure investments by 2040. Deployment costs are not included, but GETs are estimated to be cheaper than equivalent conventional reinforcements. Large-scale GET deployment would reduce demand for aluminium, copper, and steel for network assets. Challenges include lack of incentives for non-CAPEX-intensive solutions, insufficient recognition of innovation in regulation, incompatibility with current planning methodologies, operator reluctance to adopt untested technology, and “death-by-pilot” barriers to broader deployment.

normalized_text: As Europe transitions towards a more sustainable energy future the need for an expanded and modernized electricity grid becomes increasingly urgent. It is estimated that additional 80 million km of grid connections will be required worldwide by 2040 to meet decarbonisation targets, with investments amounting to trillions of euros. In this context, the present report has focused on key technologies of the grid infrastructure, including established assets (switchgear), increasingly common technologies (HVDC) and promising new approaches (GETs). The main conclusions regarding each of the analysed topics are reported below. HVDC Systems • Most of the HVDC technologies have reached operational maturity, such as converters (both VSC and LCCs) and MI and XLPE (cables at 320kV). There are still some technologies that require further validation, for instance HVDC breakers which are still maturing, XLPE cables for voltages of 525kV and higher, and HTS cables. • Europe holds ~30% of the global HVDC converter station market, with Germany (20%) and the UK (15%) leading investments. Europe is the second market player globally in HVDC converters after the Asia Pacific region. Key European companies in HVDC cables include NKT, Nexans, Süd k abe l, and Prysmian. • There is no data regarding RD&I on HVDC technologies in Europe • Europe’s HVDC Cables Market was valued at USD 4.7 billion in 2023 and is anticipated to grow at a CAGR of 19% from 2024 to 2032, complemented by the increasing focus on deployment of renewable energy sources together with grid modernization. • Europe is facing resource dependencies in relation to HVDC technology. Critical raw materials such as copper, intermediate products such as aluminium and steel, essential components such as IGBT and semi-conductors all constitute scarce resources with sovereignty risks for Europe. Furthermore, patent leadership by China (State Grid, CSG) and the U.S. (General Electric) highlights EU reliance on non-European innovators.

normalized_text: • The Technology Readiness Level for switchgears is: TRL 9 for typical MV and HV switchgears. For switchgears built with novel technologies and insulating materials with low environmental impact, like SF6-free switchgears, the technology readiness level is lower. TRL 7 – 8 for MV and HV SF6-free switchgears and TRL 5 – 6 for extra HV SF6-free switchgears. • The worldwide switchgear market is in the USD 100 billion range, with different sources giving different values and values for 2024 fluctuating from EUR 88 – 150 billion. It is estimated that it will grow to around 200 billion dollars for 2032 – 2034. • All regions, Asia Pacific, Europe, North and South America, Middle East and Asia are reporting growth in the switchgear market, with Asia Pacific expected to be the fastest growing region for the switchgear market.
statements: The technology readiness level for typical MV and HV switchgears is TRL 9.
MV and HV SF6-free switchgears have a technology readiness level of TRL 7–8, while extra HV SF6-free switchgears have a technology readiness level of TRL 5–6.
The worldwide switchgear market is estimated to grow to around 200 billion dollars for 2032–2034.
Asia Pacific is expected to be the fastest growing region in the switchgear market.

The worldwide switchgear market is in the USD 100 billion range; 2024 values vary from EUR 88–150 billion, and it is estimated to grow to around USD 200 billion by 2032–2034. normalized_text: Grid-Enhancing Technologies • GETs can potentially reduce the need for physical grid infrastructure expansion by up to 35%, alleviating supply chain issues for grid assets and delivering significant economic savings through avoided infrastructure investments. • Europe holds a leading position in the research and development of GETs, as confirmed by several metrics for scientific publications, public RD&I funding and patenting. • This leadership is generally reflected in the strong market position of the European companies, particularly in the areas of DLR and APFC. The situation is less clear for TO software, where the market and competitive landscape appear less mature. • Some barriers remain to the rapid uptake of GET solutions. The main regulatory obstacles include the lack of incentives for system operators to adopt non-CAPEX-intensive solutions, and the absence of reward mechanisms for innovation. Additional challenges include the incompatibility of GETs with some of the current planning methodologies and the recurrence of “death-by-pilot” scenarios, where it is difficult to demonstrate the transition from research to commercialization.
statements: GETs can potentially reduce the need for physical grid infrastructure expansion by up to 35%.
Europe holds a leading position in the research and development of GETs, as confirmed by metrics for scientific publications, public RD&I funding, and patenting.
The market position of European companies is strong in areas of DLR and APFC, while the market and competitive landscape for TO software appears less mature.
Regulatory obstacles to the rapid uptake of GET solutions include a lack of incentives for system operators to adopt non-CAPEX-intensive solutions and the absence of reward mechanisms for innovation.
compacted_text: GETs can potentially reduce the need for physical grid infrastructure expansion by up to 35%, easing supply chain issues for grid assets and avoiding infrastructure investments. Europe leads in GET research and development, based on scientific publications, public RD&I funding, and patenting, and this is generally reflected in strong European company positions, especially in DLR and APFC. The market for TO software is less clear and appears less mature. Barriers to rapid uptake include lack of incentives for system operators to adopt non-CAPEX-intensive solutions, absence of reward mechanisms for innovation, incompatibility with some current planning methodologies, and “death-by-pilot” scenarios where transition from research to commercialization is difficult to demonstrate.

normalized_text: Ports: the front runners of the energy transition Ports can be front runners of the energy transition. Ports provide a variety of avenues for decarbonization, from the ports themselves, the vessels that use them, heavy trucks that transports goods to and from the ports, and the surrounding industrial sites, which are often co-located with ports to benefit from easy access to bulk transportation. All stakeholders connected to a port have their own key drivers for reducing CO2 emissions. Port Authorities want to decarbonize operations, reduce energy cost and to provide a competitive gateway for attracting the maritime industry as customers. For the maritime sector it is the need for emissions reductions, in response to national and international regulations, based on for instance the IMO CO2 reduction target, and the access to compliant fuels and technologies at a competitive cost. For utilities it is providing reliable energy and heat and responding to increasing energy demand through electrification of transportation, port-related activities and industrial activities in the vicinity and increasing the value of the energy they provide to their customers. This report uses DNV GL’s Energy Transition Outlook as a basis for exploring the expected impact of Ten Key Green transitions on ports, and reveals: • The decarbonization potential in European ports including a variety of industries and water transport • How stakeholders can transform and decarbonize ports and co-located industries • The barriers and the necessary measures, including regulatory, economic and technical to unleash this potential • The policies that we recommend for accelerating the decarbonization of ports using electrification.

The report uses DNV GL’s Energy Transition Outlook to explore the expected impact of Ten Key Green transitions on ports.

normalized_text: ELECTRIFICATION OF PORT-CONNECTED ACTIVITIES: With the increased penetration of renewable energy sources such as solar PV and wind power, the carbon intensity of electricity generation will decrease significantly. Switching from fossil fuels to renewable electricity will therefore reduce carbon emissions on a global scale. There are other benefits to electrifying processes and activities. Such as, reducing local emissions and higher reliability and lower maintenance costs when using electromotors instead of internal combustion engines. Activities in ports provide ample opportunities for decarbonisation through electrification. These activities include, bunkering; logistics and freight handling with cranes and logistical vehicles; (cold) storage; service vessels, such as pilot boats and tugboats; and offices and buildings. A specific topic for ports is cold ironing, also called shore-to-ship power (SSP). The term cold ironing means that a ship docked at the port is supplied with electricity from shore and so can avoid running its engines or diesel generators to power on-board activities. Cold ironing has the benefit that local air pollution, noise and carbon emissions are reduced. Ports are also greatly affected by electrification of other sectors, such as nearby industry and electric road, river, and short distance sea transport. The electric infrastructure does not only need to facilitate the electrification of processes within the port itself, but also needs to facilitate the electrification of transport. This means that a huge capacity upgrade is required for the local electric distribution infrastructure. This requires major investments as well as sufficient space to deploy the necessary infrastructure.

Electrification of transport connected to ports requires a capacity upgrade for local electric distribution infrastructure, which needs major investments and sufficient space.

normalized_text: Ports of the future The impact of the Green Transitions will differ depending on the type of port. To quantify and illustrate the effect of the green transitions we have defined two “typical” European seaports: • A large European Industrial Port – this port is based on the average size of the 20 largest ports in Europe. It is mainly focused on bulk goods and containers. It has a large crude-oil and chemical industry cluster, co-location of power plants and a large potential for connecting offshore wind • A smaller European Transport Port – This port is one tenth of the size of the large or average industrial port and represents the average size of a seaport in Europe. It has a limited industrial cluster, mixed container and passenger transport and no offshore wind connection potential. For both seaports we have envisaged the impact of the ten green transitions in terms of energy consumption and CO2 emissions. For both ports we have defined the current situation as Port 1.0 and the future situation in 2050, as Port 2.0. Each port is characterized by five building blocks, representing the main clusters of activities that impact the energy consumption and the CO2 emissions of ports and together represent the whole port area. These building blocks are identical for all typical ports but will vary in significance and final energy use depending on the individual port. The five main building blocks, describing Port 1.0 and Port 2.0, are: PORT OPERATION Port connected activities require fuelling, e.g. for service vehicles and vessels, freight handling. FUELLLING OF TRANSPORT Fuelling of transport includes conventional oils, emerging fuels like LNG and LPG and potential new fuels like hydrogen, ammonia and electricity for hybrid or full electric ships, service vessels. It includes electrification of inland transport (e.g. heavy trucks). ELECTRICITY GENERATION This includes generation by fossil fuelled power plants, by on-site solar PV and wind turbines and connection of offshore wind parks to the public grid. INDUSTRIAL CLUSTER These include ship building and construction; chemical, metal, food and automotive industries. HYDROGEN PRODUCTION Currently this is mainly done by steam methane reforming (SMR) to serve co-located chemical industries (Grey hydrogen). Future production methods include SMR and partial oxidation (P0X) naturals gas with carbon capture and storage (CCS) resulting in blue hydrogen and electrolysis based on renewable electricity (green hydrogen).

normalized_text: Impact of Green Transitions on the power sector The electricity sector is facing major challenges in becoming carbon neutral. Decarbonization of transport will have an above average impact on electricity demand, but with the decrease in conventional power plant capacity, the need for renewable energy generation becomes more urgent. The fact that large amounts of energy from offshore wind will become available, especially near ports in the North Sea and the Baltic Sea, could make it more cost effective to use this energy near the port than to transport it inland. If the industry can benefit from cost savings in the electricity infrastructure, this will be a major driver for energy system integration in and near ports. This does however require close cooperation between the electricity sector, especially grid operators; the local industries; port authorities and terminal operators; and regulatory and permitting agencies. However, offshore wind energy will not always be fully available. To utilize this energy will require very flexible applications. As most industrial processes and applications need to run continuously, they will require an alternative energy source to switch to, such as natural gas. The most obvious applications are electric heating /steam generation and electrolysis of water to hydrogen. Both applications use natural gas as alternative fuel to switch back to during times of low variable electricity generation. Most hydrogen applications demand a continuous supply, requiring that hydrogen is buffered, or that production alternates between electrolysis and production from natural gas. This last option can benefit greatly from carbon capture and storage (CCS) infrastructure and carbon dioxide storage in depleted offshore gas fields, making continuous hydrogen production near carbon free. Hydrogen might eventually be used to produce high value fuels for sectors that are difficult to decarbonize such as aviation and shipping. With the transition to a carbon neutral energy system, there will still be a need for carbon neutral fuels that can be transported around the world to meet demand and the role ports play in supplying society with energy (whether electricity or alternative fuels) remains.

normalized_text: Electricity infrastructure and operation Within the port itself, electrification of port-connected activities through cold ironing and electrifying ferries and short-range shipping will have a major impact on the required electricity infrastructure. For example, charge poles must be installed and connected in crowded port areas. Inland transport will have a similar impact on the required infrastructure, depending to what extent road transportation will be decarbonized by electrification or by other means, like hydrogen. Most of this additional electricity demand in ports will likely have little flexibility by itself. Benefits often allocated to electrification of road transport, such as peak shaving to avoid extra cabling for which there might be little space, will need to come from additional measures, such as batteries. The landing of immense quantities of energy from offshore wind farms in the North Sea and the Baltic Sea to shore will also require huge investments. This applies not only for establishing the needed offshore grid and connections but also for the transmission of this energy to the main electricity system. It may be possible to avoid or reduce costs for investments in the transmission system through flexible solutions that use part of this energy at or near the port. The most obvious applications are electric opportunity heating and hydrogen production by electrolysis. A prerequisite however is that this industry can share in the cost savings in the transmission system, making the business case for these flexible solutions viable in ports first. This requires a clearer regulatory framework at EU and national level coupled with the right incentives to allow local demand and generation to support the networks for a representative reward, such as a local flex market where grid operators can buy flexibility to avoid congestion. This means a significant strengthening of local infrastructure. It will need to be able to facilitate huge swings in load, and special control and coordination is required as industry switches between electricity and (bio-)gas or previously stored green hydrogen, depending on the availability of electricity from offshore wind. It will require close cooperation between industry and the operation of the different infrastructures for electricity, natural gas, heat, hydrogen and possibly carbon dioxide, as all will be affected. The different infrastructures need to support each other, which leads to implications for the design as well as for operation of the energy system in and around ports. Concerns over grid reliability are often expressed due to the switch to variable renewable energy ensuring the security of electricity supply. As the system is undergoing a major change and complexity and interaction are increasing, the effect on grid reliability is uncertain. While the redundancy in the greater electricity system potentially will decrease with for the design as well as for operation of the energy system in and around ports. Concerns over grid reliability are often expressed due to the switch to variable renewable energy ensuring the security of electricity supply. As the system is undergoing a major change and complexity and interaction are increasing, the effect on grid reliability is uncertain. While the redundancy in the greater electricity system potentially will decrease with decarbonization, the redundancy of the energy system for industry around the port potentially will increase. The risks and consequences of these changes could be mitigated by a smart design of the whole system and intelligent operation and control.
statements: Electrification of port-connected activities through cold ironing and electrifying ferries and short-range shipping will have a major impact on required electricity infrastructure.
Charge poles must be installed and connected in crowded port areas.
Inland transport will have a similar impact on required infrastructure.
The impact on inland transport infrastructure depends on the extent road transportation is decarbonized by electrification or by other means like hydrogen.
Most additional electricity demand in ports is likely to have little flexibility by itself.
Benefits such as peak shaving will need to come from additional measures like batteries.
Landing immense quantities of energy from offshore wind farms to shore will require huge investments.
These investments include establishing the needed offshore grid and connections and transmitting energy to the main electricity system.
Costs for investments in the transmission system may be reduced through flexible solutions that use part of the energy at or near the port.
Electric opportunity heating and hydrogen production by electrolysis are identified as obvious applications of such flexible solutions.
A prerequisite for these flexible solutions is that the industry can share in cost savings in the transmission system.
The risks and consequences of changes to grid reliability could be mitigated by smart design of the whole system and intelligent operation and control.
compacted_text: Port electrification through cold ironing, electrifying ferries and short-range shipping, and inland transport decarbonization will significantly increase electricity infrastructure needs, including charge poles in crowded port areas. Most added port electricity demand will likely have little flexibility; peak shaving and avoiding extra cabling may require additional measures such as batteries. Landing large offshore wind energy from the North Sea and Baltic Sea will require major investments in offshore grids, connections, and transmission to the main electricity system. Flexible local use of some energy at or near the port, such as electric opportunity heating and hydrogen production by electrolysis, could reduce transmission investment costs, but this depends on a clearer EU and national regulatory framework and incentives, including a local flex market. normalized_text: Policy recommendations Ports look set to play a b1a role for EU goals on sustainability and decarbonization. However, these sustainability goals interact with other goals, such as security of energy supply and affordability of energy. This makes policy development a complex balancing act that affects all aspects of society. The nature of the ten green transitions, including their consequences, makes this even more complex. The different energy carriers and different uses of energy are increasingly interacting with each other. Successful policy will facilitate energy carriers to complement each other's weaknesses and will offer guidance to stakeholders to establish and adapt infrastructures to changing circumstances. It’s only if we use the full potential of the ten green transitions that we can realize ports as decarbonization hubs for many industries. Without the active support of all involved stakeholders, the sustainability goals and paths towards them cannot be realized. 1. Standardization of shore power should be stimulated and barriers to adhere to the standards should be removed Some standards for shore power have been established, while more are in process for new technologies. Still these standards are not yet fully accepted. Some suppliers of charging equipment—especially for ferries—do not adhere to it, and instead opt for more automated and tailored solutions to reduce connection time and to save on handling cost. When their facilities are specifically designed for specific individual ferries, this can prevent other ships from using these facilities, possibly limiting the potential of these facilities later. The use of the existing standards and evolution of new standards should be further promoted. Ports belonging to the Trans European Transport Network (TEN-T) already have to implement shore power facilities by 2025 according to the existing standard for High Voltage. It is recommended to expand this to standards in other domains and promote the use of the standards in other ports. 2. Stimulate further electrification of port-connected activities for early movers The general conclusion of most innovative electrification projects designed, developing and testing installations and vehicles powered by electricity, seems to be that these alternatives are well suited for the assigned tasks. Follow-up investments are rarely made because of the high investments in charging infrastructure, initial lack of customers that will use it, and the current limited number of suppliers for equipment (comparable to the EV market 5 to 10 years ago). Interventions like funds for the unprofitable top, buy-back arrangements and accelerated depreciation should be considered to compensate first movers to implement existing, or soon to be available standards. TEN-T ports already must implement shore power facilities by 2025 under the existing High Voltage standard; this should be expanded to other standards and other ports. normalized_text: • Resilient grids: The DC grid can support the resilience of the AC utility grid. For example, energy storage connected to the DC grid can ingest energy from or feed energy back into the utility grid depending on the current demand/supply situation. Additionally, they provide peak-power for short-time needs reducing the in-feed power and easing the load on the supply grid. Decentralized grid management, simple integration of storage units and a single connection to the public AC grid ensure a reliable power supply. • Increased energy efficiency: Industrial applications are driven by about 70% by frequency converters which convert electricity twice: from 50 Hz AC on the supply grid to direct current and then from that direct current to a variable frequency for efficient operation of motors and drives. Connecting all DC links of the inverters with each other makes the first AC-DC conversion redundant. This also allows for direct use of the breaking energy from moving masses, leading to energy savings of six to ten percent, depending on the process dynamics. • Efficient integration of renewable energies: Since solar power generation systems supply direct current, all that is needed is a DC/DC converter instead of the much more costly DC/AC inverter. This not only saves costs, but also improves dynamics and efficiency. • DC grids are safe: The DC grid is just as safe for people as today's AC grid, as dedicated protective devices ensure the safety of people and equipment during operation and in the event of faults.

heading_path: Main > 1. Introduction > General
normalized_text: The increasing focus on energy efficiency and sustainability has accelerated the shift towards alternative energy sources as part of broader decarbonisation efforts. This has led to a growing share of renewable energy sources (RES) in the electricity mix, prompting a move away from the traditional centralised grid model toward more flexible and decentralised approaches. At the same time, the rise in direct current (DC) electricity demand—driven by technologies such as LED lighting, digital devices, electric vehicles, and energy storage systems—has highlighted the potential of medium- and low-voltage (MV and LV) DC grids. These grids offer a promising pathway to deliver electricity that is not only safer and more efficient, but also aligned with the goals of a green and resilient energy transition. The integration of these systems with existing AC grids should leverage the distinct advantages of each grid type. This approach will support the gradual development of hybrid networks across the European Union, ensuring they meet the evolving needs of a decarbonised energy system. It is, however, crucial to maintain reliability, stability, cost-efficiency, and security of supply throughout this transition. The challenge for integrating more renewable energy and DC loads is therefore to seamlessly integrate DC grids into the main electricity network while ensuring resiliency and cost effectiveness. The challenge for integrating more renewable energy and DC loads is therefore to seamlessly integrate DC grids into the main electricity network while ensuring resiliency and cost effectiveness. Benefits: -reduction in primary energy consumption; -grid power quality; - Higher power transmission with identical wire cross-sections; - easier integration of alternative energy sources; - fewer components and therfore lower capital costs; - longer life expectancy of appliances; - Convenience, aesthetics and reduction of weight and space requirements; - Seamless development of smart grids; - Easier integration. Barriers: Standardisation, Regulation, Safety, Market ,Technology.
